description:
A card game for 2-6 players involving low cunning with high science. Race to complete your blueprint before your rival mad inventors!

Set against the backdrop of the Victorian era, the year is 1899.  You are racing against your rival inventors to be the first to build your invention for the 1900 World's Fair.  Scavenge junk for components, sabotage your competitors, and build the greatest invention the world has even known!  Be the mad genius you always knew you could be.

Collect each of the 5 components to complete your blueprint from the market place pile.  Collect the rare Electric Generator, the uncommon Compressed Gases, or the ever present Dials & Gauges. Each turn you may use your Action cards to scavenge the junk pile for components, sabotage your rivals, or hire lab assistants! Be the first inventor to collect all the components needed to complete your blueprint to win.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description: Each player is dealt a hand of Chinese Zodiac sign cards. The current player plays a card that must be better than the previously played card, according to a specified order such as speed, weight, etc. The person who gets rid of their hand first is the winner!

Super Smart (or Supersmart) is a game for 2-4 players from 9 years and up.

In this game you are are answering questions in one word beginning with a specific, but random letter.

The board consist with four independent lanes from each with a "Start" field and a passage of extra 10 steps. In the middle there is a roulette with letters (and small numbers) and six (6) coloured spots; Four (4) red ones and two (2) green ones. The game also contains four (4) player pawns (Red, Green, Blue and Yellow), one (1) die (Red with golden eyes) and a deck of Question Cards (from now called CQs).

The CQs are suffled and placed face down in reach of all players.
The youngest player starts the game by pushing the arrow of the roulette and there are following three possibility:
1) If the arrow stops at a letter the player takes a CQ and read up the question for the player on the right, who answer the question with a word starting with the letter from the roulette. The amount of time that you have to reply is agreed before the game starts. If the answer is approved the player is allowed to move one (1) step up the ladder - if not the player is not moving.
After this all other players roll the die - the player who first roll the number under the letter, can also move his/her pawn one (1) step up the ladder
2) Destiny / Red field - if the arrow stops here, all players roll the die and the player who rolls the number shown on the roulette will move his/her pawn one (1) step down the ladder.
3) Luck / Green field - If the arrow stops here, the player who spins the wheel can move his/her pawn two (2) steps up the ladder.
If the arrow stops between two fields, the player do a respin.

The first to reach the top of the ladder is the winner!

Find easiest way to move matches in the form on the card.
Players try simultaneously find the lowest number of moves to transform matches according to next picture. One saying lowest number (and showing moves) get the point, player who collect most points win the game. The game ends when all cards are gone through.

Two titans appear on the horizon. The behemoths quickly engage in battle, seeming to blindly attack each other with no care for the buildings around them. In fact, they seem to delight in the destruction left in their wakes. But there can be only one victor. Will it be you?

Mega Monster City Smash is a remote controlled monster city smashing game. In phase one, players build their city buildings using strategy to pick their build sites. Then, in phase two, they send their giant monsters into the city and smash their opponent's buildings before their own buildings meet the same fate. Fight off the opposing monster and keep your buildings from being reduced to rubble to achieve victory!

boardgame: Who Wants To Be a Millionaire: Hot Seat – The Board Game
description:
FROM THE BOX:

Who Wants to Be a Millionaire? Hot Seat is a fast-paced, tension-filled TV game show - a contest of chance, strategy and wits. Now you can enjoy all the excitement of the show with this fun family board game edition.

There's 2 ways to play and hundreds of fun and interesting questions that will delight and challenge your family and friends many times over.

So get everyone together and see who can survive the Hot Seat!

Each player has a circular playing piece with six pie-shaped holes. The goal of the game is to collect a pie in each color. The colors correspond to different question categories.

The board consists of a circular track with spaces in seven different colors. Six of the colors correspond to question categories while the last color gives a new dice roll. Six spaces along the track are "pie spaces", and from these there are "spokes" of track leading to the middle of the board.

Players roll a die and move along the track in any direction they like. When a player stops on a color they get a question of the appropriate category. If the player answers a question correctly while on a pie space, they get a pie of that color (assuming they don't already have it). A correct answer on another square allows the player to roll again.

Once the player has one pie in each color, she can move along the spokes to the middle of the board to win the game.

Each player gets 5 event cards and place them year-side down on the table.
Now the first player takes the top event card from the deck and place it year-side up on the table and start laying the first serie on the table either before or after the drawn card. Other players follow him similar way placing the cards before, after of between the cards on the serie.

In a player's turn, if he suspects that there is an error on the table, he may accuse the previous players. All cards are turned over one by one and checked the chronology. If everything is OK the current player draw a new event card as a penalty. If something is wrong the last player draw a new card.
The new serie starts.

Who gets rid of their cards first is the winner.

Thematic edition of Trivial Pursuit.

In Time-Travelling Robot Bakery, you are a programmer who works with robots in a bakery, and it's your job to make sure the robots are working as quickly as possible. These robots are capable of time travel, mind you, so as you battle for ingredients and supplies, it's possible for your opponent to go back in time and get to them before you do. If you can bake a cake with your robots before your opponent can, you win!

In the game, players program four robots to move around a modular board and to try to gather ingredients for a cake. The robots are added to the board one by one, as they duplicate via time travel, creating a parallel algorithm of growing complexity. Coordinating the movements as you add more and more robots to the picture is key to solving this puzzle-y game fastest. The game board is created from double-sided cards, allowing for varying gameplay each session with different options on the different sides of the cards.

boardgame: TSWW: Operation Merkur – The Invasion of Crete, 1941
description:
Description from the publisher:

Welcome to Crete!

Not quite what the New Zealand, Australian, Greek and British troops said to the German Fallschirmjaeger as they descended to Crete in May of 1941.

Operation Merkur is the new TSWW mini-game which deals with the Axis invasion of Crete, using a cut-down set of rules, half month and five-day turns. as well as including for the first time a competitive play rule using optional units to tailor force structures.

description:

A different edition was released as Trivial Pursuit: TV Edition – 4,800 Questions and Answers.

JUX, a story building game for creative thinkers, is a card game for 2-8 players that lets you all become co-authors of your own wacky, where's-it-going tale. The goal of JUX is to create a story by building a collaborative, run-on sentence, inspired by the quirky illustrations. The game takes 5 minutes to learn, and lasts 30 minutes or longer (depending on how much fun you are having).

Each round begins by revealing a new Objective card and choosing "the best way" or "the worst way" to pursue it. Each player in turn then adds a sentence fragment, using one of their images cards, to extend or to improve the current story - playing off of what others have played.

A player just created a hilarious twist to the story? Give them a Appreciation Token for their creative contribution. Someone's new addition sounds like the best way to accomplish the objective, instead of the worst way? Challenge their card, and make a convincing argument to your friends. Branch the story onto a new path to take the tale in a new direction. Think up a creative ending to the collaborative story to finish the round. All will gain you Appreciation Token, an acknowledgement for your creative thinking throughout play. Collect the most Appreciation Token to become the Best Storyteller!

Specifically designed with younger children in mind. Recommended for ages 5 and up; BrainBox: Dinosaurs is recommended for ages 8 and up.

Players will have 10 seconds to study a card before being asked a question from the back, chosen by the roll of the die. If the question is answered correctly the card is kept, if not it is returned to the box. The player with the most cards after 10 minutes wins the game!

The object of the game is to study a card for 10 seconds and then answer a question based on the roll of a die. If the question is answered correctly, the card is kept, and the person with the most number of cards after 5 or 10 minutes is the winner.

From publisher:

This exciting addition to our BrainBox range enables children to create their own unique BrainBox game. Each card shows a different scene as a line drawing, ready for children to colour in. Coloured pencils are included, but felt-tips could also be used. Some of the questions on the cards will have different answers, depending on the child's choice of colours, and the final question is left for children to write themselves. Encourage your child's creativity, memory and observation skills with this great game.

The three scenes makes and act where the Stranger must lead the citizens against the Bad guys who terrorize the town. When the job is done the stranger rides into the setting sun, if he survives.

Genericorp's experiments opened the rift at the bottom of the of the Helles Sea, horrific monsters are pouring through, and you need to fight them back! Attack on Mars is a co-op game for 2-4 players. Choose your pilot, your corporation, and your Mecha. Upgrade your Mecha while defending cities and fighting monsters. Research how to close the rift and then fight the Guardian in order to do so. Win, and Mars is safe. For now.

Pontoons is played on a 6x10 grid of rectangular cells; these are almost square but a little shortened on the longest side of the board. The board is oriented with a side of six cells next to each player. These nearest six cells are called the “bank,” and to start the game, each player has six pieces called “men” in his own color, one on each cell. In the next two rows, referred to as a “mooring,” each player starts with twelve “boat” pieces of his own color. These pieces are large enough for the “men” to easily stand upon.

Players take turns moving one piece at a time, man or boat. Men may move any number of spaces vertically, horizontally, or diagonally, but not back towards their own bank. Between banks, men may only move about on or over boats. Only one man may ride a boat at a time. Boats move in the same manner as men. Men may ride a boat of either color, but the boat’s rules of movement do not change. Men may not be removed from a boat of their own color, but may be thrown overboard from one of their opponent’s boats.

Men may throw the other player’s men overboard by landing on or jumping over their boat. To do this, the opponent’s man must be in an adjacent cell; the capturing player must land on that boat or land on an empty boat opposite. A man thrown overboard returns to any empty cell on his player’s bank and begins again.

When a boat and man reach the opponent’s mooring, they are permanently removed from the board. The first player to remove all his own pieces from the board wins the game.

Merchant Marine is a strategic board game of free-for-all cargo recovery and competitive naval maneuvering. Players race to collect goods from a sinking vessel and return them to the appropriate port, but must stay out of range of their rivals' hungry cannons in the process.

Recovered goods can be returned to any port for a small number of points, or they can be taken to that good's matching port for a large number of points. You can stockpile as many goods as you want before returning to port, but taking on too many goods will reduce your vessel's speed. Players must be realistic about the port they can reach before their rivals are upon them.

The ships in Merchant Marine act like large ships should -- they are hard to stop and cannot turn on a dime. Players must wrestle with these limitations even while exploiting the same weaknesses in their rivals. Run down another player to steal their precious cargo for yourself, or raise your sails in a desperate bid to outrun a more encumbered vessel.

The player with the most points when the final load of goods has been recovered from the sinking ship is the winner.

In Business Mogul you play as the leader of a rising corporate empire, competing with your peers to amass the most power. Game play is centered around building an economic engine, and uses a bidding mechanic where players must balance bids to get the good stuff and to avoid the bad against the actions needed to build their empire, while saving high bids as much as possible to convert to cash at the end of each season.

The game has a fixed number of turns - six seasons of five rounds each - and total play time tends to be about 2-2.5 hours for 4 people (the game is designed for 3-5, with a solid 2-player variant), on par with a feature movie. In every round there is one card per player up for grabs - every player gets a card every round. The cards on which players bid are businesses that are used to build up your economic engine, extra actions to be saved until needed most (and they let a player do some things outside the normal rules of play), special agenda cards that give score a bonus at the end of the game (how much depends on how well the conditions for the given card are fulfilled), and bad events (which are to be avoided if possible).

Players are bidding pick order rather than on a particular item. Each players uses a set nine of bidding cards (getting them all back at the start of each season), which represent how the player is dividing their attention that round between trying to get the best of the available items and getting other stuff done. Each bid has a specific action that it allows (lower bids have more powerful actions), and bids saved for the end of the season are worth extra money - the higher the bid the more it’s worth. These additional aspects associated with each bid, the action and the end-of-season cash value, make for exciting, meaningful choices every round.

Business Mogul supports multiple valid strategies, though the highest final score usually goes to the player that dabbles in additional strategies beyond their primary one. The key to winning is being tactically smart about balancing pick order vs action vs end-of-season-payout, paying attention to the state of other player’s empires and the bids they've already used in the current season, and executing a strategy that adapts to the flow of items up for bid.

There are some fairly common game mechanics/attributes that Mogul specifically does not have:

- It has no player-to-player aggression - the bidding each round is the only point of direct competition. 
- It does not have random action outcomes (that is whether or not a given action succeeds is not determined by dice, card draw, or anything like that). 
- It does not have any kind of territory control. 
- It has no resources / production beyond money and end-game score.
- It does not require memorization of what’s been played; aside from a single hidden card each player gets at the start and the particular bid a player is making in a given round, the state of the game is fully revealed.

Back of the book:

1945, GERMANY... the heart of Germany is threatened by the remorseless advance of Allied armies from the east and west. From the borders of France, Belgium and Holland, US and British armies fight their way through the West Wall and on to the river Rhine, then over it and into Germany's industrial heartlands. In Poland, the unstoppable tide of the Red Army sweeps across the Vistula river and strikes for the Oder, beyond which lies the ultimate objective - Berlin, and the final bitter battles of the Second World War in Europe.

BACKGROUND
A general history of the war from Germany's borders to the streets of Cologne, Konigsberg, Bremen and Berlin, covering the period from January 1945 to the ultimate defeat of the Third Reich in May.

ARMY LISTS
Four army lists designed to play with the character of the principal combatants: German 'Defenders of the Reich', to create the ad hoc battlegroups thrown to fight the invasion. These scratch units, mixing a few veteran survivors with Volksturm and armed civilians, must face the power of Russian, British and American armoured spearheads as they drive ever deeper into Germany.

Also game data for all the late war tanks, guns and equipment.

SPECIAL RULES
New special rules to adapt core Battlegroup game to the battlefields of 1945. The rules recreate the desperate last stand nature of much of the fighting in 1945 whilst retaining game balance, despite the German Army's collapsing morale. Special rules include a new battle counters set as well as new innovations like jet fighters and infra-red sights.

SCENARIOS
Four new general scenarios, including an 'Urban Assault' and a mini-campaign 'Breakthrough to Berlin', of six linked historical refights set on the Seelow Heights.

description:
Block Party is a game that delights and challenges active minds. Players race to find patterns of icons called Parties. Parties are highly symmetric collections of icons which tickle the brain. With simple shapes and pleasing colors, Block Party brings out your playful side and gets your wheels spinning from the first "Party!"

Features gameplay borrowing attribute matching mechanics like those found in Set and Qwirkle, a tiny number of components (can be played with only 8 wooden blocks), and dozens of variations to vary the game duration, number of players, cooperation or competition, and age range (easier variations for young players and harder ones for masters).

How to Play: Block Party Classic

Block Party can be played with 1-6 players for as long as they like. Players simultaneously search for parties (symmetric collections of four icons, see below for more), shouting "Party!" when they find one, after which they put it together to show the group and flip the four blocks used.

For a more finite and competitive version, players can keep score (often with tokens or Block Party cards) where the first player to 10 parties wins. This number can be changed to alter the duration of the game, or set per player to handicap and make for a fair race.

Rock Science: Festival is a travel version of rock and metal trivia game Rock Science. The game covers hundreds of bands, from Classic Rock to Nu Metal via Trash Metal and many others.

Players compete to collect most point chips through answering questions. Question categories include rockers, albums and songs, and players may also be asked to hum riffs. All questions have three different difficulty levels - the higher difficulty a player chooses, the higher the possible reward in point chips. Other important game mechanics are betting (you constantly bet on the knowledge of co-players).

The game includes question cards (around 800 questions in total), die, point/betting chips and rules.

Find out which robot in the Robosphere has what it takes with this custom paper-crafting kit! Flex your crafting muscles and create 10 easy-to-assemble robots, each with their own personality and fighting style. Pair up any two 'bots to battle head-to-head by placing each atop the included motorized wheel bases. See who knocks the other off the fighting ring first, and keep score on the Robodrome's scoreboard. This kit also features your very own Robot Wars Combat Manual with fun background stories and directions on how to construct each rough-and-tumble machine.

Kit includes full-color templates to construct 10 paper-craft robots, 2 wind-up wheel frames, deluxe fighting ring with scoreboards, and a step-by-step instruction book complete with details on each robot's personality.

The Chase is played on a square wooden board with a round pit, the "hideout," carved out of the center.  Four lines of small holes extend vertically and horizontally from the pit to the sides of the board, and continue around all four sides making a border.  Near each corner of the board are four more small holes.  Printed on the board over the lines of small holes are the outlines of footprints leading in one direction, ultimately towards the hideout.  Printed over the groupings of four holes are a bomb, a noose, a gun, and a knife.

Four players have four pegs each in their own color.  Play begins with their four pieces resting in one of the four corner groupings.  Players take turns rolling a six-sided die to determine the length of their move.  Any one of the player's pegs may be moved, but most be moved the exact number of footprints rolled on the die.

If landing on a hole occupied by another player, the first peg is returned to its home base to begin again, and the peg in play is moved five extra spaces.  Players may not land on a footprint occupied by one of their own pegs; they must choose to move a different peg.

The hideout may only be reached by an exact roll of the die.  If no other moves are possible, that player forfeits the turn and the peg remains in place.  The first player to move all four pegs into the hideout wins the game.

Super Bis is pretty simple language learning game. The game comprises of two decks of cards (one deck of questions and one deck of answers). For every question card, there is just one matching answer card. There is also one joker and one jack-in-the box -card. There is two rule sets in the game.

In the first game you just deal out the cards. Joker is removed. Everybody then removes matching pairs from their hands. Then the player in turn takes a card from the previous player. If the card matches a card in the players hand, he/she reads it loud and removes the pair. Then it's next players turn. This goes on until cards run out. Winner is the player who gets rid of his/her cards first and the loser is the player holding the pairless jack-in-the-box when pairs run out.

In the other game you just deal out the question cards. Joker and the jack-in-the-box are removed. Answer cards are shuffled and put in the table. One player then turns up the first card from the answers deck and reads it out. The player with the matching question reads it and then discards it. Then you read out the next answer card. Winner is the player who first gets rid of his/her cards.

Objective: be the first one getting rid of all your pieces
 - acting by turns -
Each player at the start of the game has a set of pieces in one colour. Two dice direct a.) the tray & b.) how many playing pieces must be placed on the waiters’ trays.
Playing pieces may be placed in any order except for the coffee pot and the cheese dish which must be the last items.
The six waiters are positioned in such a way that as one falls, the next waiter may also tumble. Dropped pieces must be taken of whatever colour and add them to your own.

Note:
This is not the same game as Don't Tip the Waiter, Don't Tip the Waiter  or Don't Tip The Waiter.

Bible Wars: the board game, takes the tactical level Bible Wars Card Game to the next level.

The game features the same 6 cultures on a strategic map of the Holy Lands.  In the game each culture is trying to capture and hold onto 18 territories to win but, it's not easy.

The first player each turn is randomly determined. Combat is determined, not only by who has the most armies, but by terrain and the playing of cultural army cards.  The cards give each side an advantage to add to their die roll. Combat can continue several rounds until the attacked retreats or the defender is eliminated. There is no retreat for the defender.

Cultural advantages include Egyptian Chariots, Philistine Giants, Babylonian War Elephants, Israeli Heavenly Interventions, Canaanite Additional Local Forces, and Assyrian Professional Units.
family: History: Wars in the Ancient Near East

boardgame: Tilt: The Star of Card Games
description:
Tilt is a self-published card game system which utilizes a 66-card deck. The cards are divied into eight suits (A, B, F, L, O, R, S, U) with eight cards per suit (HIGH, DOUBLE, 4, 3, 2, 1, TWIN, LOW). Two other cards are included, the ROYAL E and a WILD card, each of which have a rank of 0 when necessary.

The 34 page rulebook describes seven games:
Regular Tilt
Reverse Tilt
Double Tilt
Royal Tilt
Chain Letter
Vocab
Tilt Crossword Solitaire

The Tilt family of games are trick-taking games where certain ranks are worth 1 or 2 points. One suit per player, plus one extra for a dummy hand, are used. Players bid for the right to name a trump suit, and the winner draws two cards from the dummy to improve his hand (and discarding two unwanted cards). The different versions change the rules or scoring in various ways.

Chain Letter is a stops-style game where players try to discard their hand by matching the last-played card. Only suits count, not ranks.

Vocab is a spelling game where players use the cards as letters, trying to make three-letter words. The TWIN cards count as two letters, so TWIN L is LL, TWIN A is AA, etc.

Tilt Crossword Solitaire is as it sounds, a solitaire game where the player fills an 8 by 8 grid with cards. Points are earned for letters in words or abbreviations, while they are lost for empty spaces in the grid.

The game and rulebook are copyright 1946 by William H. Mulder. The place of publication is Grand Rapids, Michigan. No manufacturer is listed.

Bolster your armies with Colossal Warfare, the third expansion for Warmachine: High Command!

Colossal Warfare contains 5 copies each of 16 different cards, plus 4 additional warcaster cards, to bring new strategies to your Cygnar, Khador, Protectorate of Menoth, and Cryx decks.

Requires Warmachine: High Command core set to play.

■Idea of the GamePlayers play Hunter cards (Grim Reaper, Angel, and Devil cards) to fight over Soul cards.
Players fight over Soul cards on the table by playing Hunter cards from their hand. The player who has collected Soul cards of all 3 types, 3 Soul cards of the same type, or 5 Soul cards of any type wins the game.

				
				
					■How to playPlay a Hunter card from your hand face down on the Angel or Devil team side.
After all players pass their turns or the maximum number of Hunter cards playable (as shown on the Soul card) are played, flip all played Hunter cards face up. 
Add the strength of the cards on each team side (Angel and Devil) to calculate the total strength of each team.
The player who played the Hunter card with the lowest number in the team with higher strength shall receive the Soul card of that round.

				
				
					■PointIn this game, you need to play a high-number card(s) to form a majority and a low-number card(s) to outdo other players in the majority.
The point of this game lies on how you handle this dilemma.
publishers: さとーふぁみりあ (Sato Familie)

description:
GenesisThe dawn of intergalactic travel is so close that its electric body galvanizes the minds of thousands of scientists and millions of wannabe colonists.

Quantum engineering, 3D printing and artificial intelligence are pervasive branches of science, intertwined with every aspect of daily life. Everybody has access to them, everybody uses them.

The graviton is tamed and has sent the solar system abuzz, teeming with ships, robots and people.

But the dark side of humanity is showing its claws. The human civilization has been polarized by the people who want to acquire and maintain their power over the masses. The earthlings and the spacelings of this world are in conflict.

One man has breached the very wheelwork of Nature and has found a way to go anywhere in an instant, jumping through space as if walking through open doors between worlds.

The leaders of the United Planets Alliance are trying to find this man and obtain control over the greatest achievement of mankind: the obliteration of any barriers of space.

It is your destiny to intervene and set mankind on its path to the future you wish for.

Join your forces with others like you, breathe life into your faction, take part in events happening in the solar system and beyond, and bring ruin or triumph to a world which finds itself in the agony of childhood.

No man or woman has ever been closer to the future of this world. Reach up and embrace your destiny, for you have the power to bring change!




				
				
					DescriptionThe Gardener of Thoughts Card Game (GOT CG) is a competitive card game for 2 players, with a science fiction theme and atmospheric lore.

The game can be played by casual players right out of the box with excellent balance. There is a single deck of cards, no other play components. Advanced players may add custom cards.

The main mechanics are: shuffled cards, card drawing, turns, card playing, abilities (whose rules are applied when cards are played), and combo actions (= combining the abilities of multiple cards played in a single turn).

The system's logic is based on the diversity of the (rules of the) abilities present on the cards, and has an emphasis on combo actions. Combo actions emerge fluidly from the abilities, and have to be created on the spot by the players. To master the game, a player has to master combo actions.

The typical game duration for veteran players, without advanced gameplay, is (consistently) 30 minutes. Beginner players (who already know the rules) need about 60 minutes.




				
				
					Gameplay SynopsisMost of the time, a player has to engage (= play from hand) during his / her turns cards whose total sway (= numeric card value) is at least equal with the total sway of the cards engaged by the rival, thusly obstructing the rival.

Normally, during a turn, a player may engage only 1 card, but the abilities of the engaged cards allow for more cards to be engaged or drawn. This multiple engagement of cards is called combo action and forms the game's logic. Combo actions with 10 cards are not rare, and even with more than 20 have been seen.

From time to time, a player will not be able to obstruct the rival because he / she will not be able to create combos that are strong enough. In such a case, the player must discard 1 card from his / her reserve. Reserve is a play zone of each player, zone which contains a number of cards which is fixed at the beginning of the game; the number is normally 3, but advanced players can increase it by recruiting (= choosing) cards at the beginning of the game.

By discarding a reserve card each time when a player can't obstruct the rival, the player will have fewer cards to engage during the last turn of the game, the turn which will determine the winner of the game. However, simply having more or fewer cards in the reserve at the end of the game doesn't guarantee winning or losing the game, it just increases or decreases the chances. This is because each player also has some random cards in his / her hand, and this randomness affects the ending.

Theme
Players take the part of land owners, attempting to buy and then develop their land. Income is gained by other players visiting their properties and money is spent when they visit properties belonging to other players. When times get tough, players may have to mortgage their properties to raise cash for fines, taxes and other misfortunes.

Gameplay
On his turn, a player rolls two dice and moves that number of spaces around the board. If the player lands on an as-yet-unowned property, he has the opportunity to buy it and add it to his portfolio or allow the bank to auction it to the highest bidder. If a player owns all the spaces within a color group, he may then build houses and hotels on these spaces, generating even more income from opponents who land there. If he lands on a property owned by another player, he must pay that player rent according to the value of the land and any buildings on it. There are other places on the board which can not be bought, but instead require the player to draw a card and perform the action on the card, pay taxes, collect income, or even go to jail.

Goal
The goal of the game is to be the last player remaining with any money.

A re-theme of Monopoly made for Waterhout Construction Company of St. Louis. The company has been involved in a number of commercial and residential projects over the years, including the Puffin and Penguin House at the St. Louis Zoo as well as other projects throughout the country.

11" x 11" wooden box with sliding lid.

boardgame: South-East Sweden 1925 (fan expansion for Ticket to Ride)
description:
South-East Sweden 1925 is an unofficial expansion to Alan R Moon’s and Days of Wonder's Ticket to Ride series.  The map covers the southeastern part of Sweden and can be played by 2-5 players.

The goal for this game is to collect points by claiming routes, completing destination tickets, completing more tickets than the opponents, and building the longest continuous route.


NOTE: "Ticket to Ride" and "Days of Wonder" are registered trademarks of Days of Wonder, Inc. This fan-created expansion map is not a Days of Wonder product, but has been created with permission of Days of Wonder, Inc, solely for unpaid, non-commercial use. Some of the text and illustrations used herein may be Copyright © 2004-2014, Days of Wonder, Inc. All Rights Reserved.

boardgame: Magic: The Gathering Challenge Deck – Battle the Horde
description:
From the back of the "Face the Hydra" challenge deck, "This box contains sixty challenge cards and an instructional playmat. To play against this challenge deck, each player must have a Magic: The Gathering deck."

The challenge deck allows 1 person or multiple people working together to play against a programmed deck. The cards in this deck are not designed to be mixed with standard M:tG cards and have a distinct back. Players play their cards against this deck before running out of health. The challenge deck does not play cards normally. Each turn a card is drawn and its effect is applied immediately.

In Battle the Horde, the player(s) are facing a rampaging army of Minotaurs.  They must defeat all the cards in the deck to win.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

boardgame: Magic: The Gathering Challenge Deck – Defeat a God
description:
From the back of the "Face the Hydra" challenge deck, "This box contains sixty challenge cards and an instructional playmat. To play against this challenge deck, each player must have a Magic: The Gathering deck."

The challenge deck allows 1 person or multiple people working together to play against a programmed deck. The cards in this deck are not designed to be mixed with standard M:tG cards and have a distinct back. Players play their cards against this deck before running out of health. The challenge deck does not play cards normally. Each turn a card is drawn and its effect is applied immediately.

Ultimate Battle challenges players to use creativity and think on their feet. The arena: a sheet of paper set between the players. The fuel: Ultimate Battle cards and a good, thick marker! Do you have what it takes to out-think, out-maneuver, and out-sketch your opponents? Combine cards, launch devastating attacks, and defend against a barrage of creativity!

In Ultimate Battle, the goal is to utterly destroy your opponent. In this creativity-fueled game it's one-hit destruction, so players must plan attacks and defenses carefully.

The gameplay centers around two to four players each drawing from a deck of Ultimate Battle Cards. These fully illustrated cards contain everything from powerful weapons and creatures (like sharks and poison) to more mundane objects (like a brick). Using their imaginations, players must illustrate attacks against their opponents on a large sheet of paper rolled out between both players. The attacking player uses any combination of cards in their hand to launch their attack. The defending player must then use cards from their hand to defend.

If players disagree on whether an attack or defense is valid a Disagreement Card is drawn, which quickly resolves any qualms. This gets the group back to playing as quickly as possible.

If a player can't defend an attack, or loses a disagreement when trying to defend, he or she is destroyed! Whether a world-class artist or a stick-figure impresario, everyone can draw out wild attacks in Ultimate Battle. Think quick, sketch even quicker, and may the best battler win!

Players in During Spring, Summer and Winter need to determine which card has been removed from the deck, and to do that they'll need to ask others which cards they hold in hand — but not directly.

The deck consists of four cards for each season numbered 1, 1, 2 and 3, along with a "rainy season" card when playing with four players. At the start of a round, shuffle the cards, place one in the center of the table, then distribute the rest of the cards to the players. On a turn, a player asks one of two types of questions of another player: "How many cards do you hold of a certain number?" or "What's the sum of the spring (summer, etc.) cards that you hold?" In the latter case, as a cost you must place  one card from your hand on display, revealing information to all players.

When you think you know which card is in the middle of the table, take a peek; if you're correct, you score points and the game ends as soon as someone has collected six points.

This 56-page sourcebook, written by Neil Smith, chronicles the devastating blow suffered by the Romans in the Teutoberger Wald at the hands of the Germanic tribes led by Arminius in AD 9. The battle, also known by the Romans as the Varian Disaster, saw the Germans ambush and destroy three Roman legions and their supporting troops, ending Roman territorial expansion across the Danube.

This book provides army lists and special rules for the Germanic tribes, and scenario rules to describe both the battle and its aftermath.

boardgame: Dux Britanniarum: The Raiders – The Picts, Scotti and Irish in the Age of Arthur
description:
The Raiders for Dux Britanniarum is an expansion to the main rules which introduces three new factions to the game; the Irish, the Scotti and the Picts as well as rule variations for the British Kingdoms of the North, the Gwyr Y Gogledd.

This 44-page supplement features rules to cover the new factions, as well as beautiful colour maps of Ireland and Scotland along with campaign rules for the Raider nations.

Game description from the publisher:

Prescience is a two-player, action-based time travel board game. The goal of Prescience (as determined by which scenario you play) may be simple, but things become more complicated each time the players travel back in time to relive the opening of the game.

Players always control the most current version of themselves, but they have the opportunity to use their knowledge of the past to interact with their former selves — or the former versions of their opponent. Through special abilities such as pushes, pulls, and stuns, they can change the course of the previous generations. However, each time they change the past, the effects of that change continue to ripple forward as the game progresses. Players who are able to use their knowledge of the past to their advantage will be victorious in Prescience!

Bypass is played an a 12x12 square grid of circles, all of which are connected horizontally and vertically.  There are four areas of the board in two shades each of red, blue, yellow, and green.  On two opposite sides of the board is a black border numbered one through five, then skipping two spaces, and numbered five through one.  A similar red numbered border exists on the board's other sides.

The game is played with up to six pieces for each player in red, blue, yellow, or green.  The object is for a player to start the game with their pieces in the opposite corner of the home area of their color, and to move at least three of their pieces into that home area before their opponents, to win the game.

Movement of pieces is determined by one red and one black six-sided die; a piece will be moved to the spot where these two coordinates meet.  There then follows a series of rules governing the exact part of the board the piece can move to, and what goals must be met for the piece to move further, and what happens when pieces become "triangulated," or captured.

Notes: The rules are detailed but something less than clear on some important details.  Also, like other games from this publisher, the game board is a printed sheet of paper held between a transparent sheet of plastic in a black plastic frame.

The board for Run Sheepee Run begins with a 3x3 square grid of nine holes, with four similar grids branching out from the corner holes of the center square, for a total of 41 holes.  One black marble (the wolf) and twelve white marbles (the sheep) are included with the game, and rest easily in the board's holes.

To start the game, the wolf places his marble anywhere on the board.  The sheep then places a marble anywhere on the board. The wolf may begin moving to one adjacent hole in any direction.  The sheep must continue placing his marbles one at a time anywhere on the board until they have all been placed.  At that point, the sheep marbles can move the same as the wolf.

The wolf can capture any sheep in an adjacent hole by jumping over it to a vacant hole on its opposite side and removing it from play.  Multiple jumps are possible.  The sheep cannot capture but must try to crowd the wolf so that it cannot move, either with white marbles or against the edge of the board.  If the sheep trap the wolf, they win.  If the wolf can no longer be trapped, he wins.

This is a variant of Fox and Geese.

boardgame: The Wheel of Time Collectible Card Game: Dark Prophecies
description: The first expansion to the Wheel of Time CCG, Dark Prophecies consists of 154 cards (50 common, 50 uncommon, 54 rares), distributed in 9 card booster packs. This expansion introduces a new card type, Prophecies, allowing players to establish and then fulfill Prophecies in order to gain a major advantage over their opponent.

boardgame: The Wheel of Time Collectible Card Game: Children of the Dragon
description: The second expansion to the Wheel of Time CCG, Chidren of the Dragon consists of 150 cards (50 common, 50 uncommon, 50 rares), distributed in 9 card booster packs. This expansion contains new characters and troops for all allegiances in the game including Children of the Light, Aes Sedai, Andoran, Dark One, and Dragon, but with a major focus on the Aiel, featuring all twelve clan chiefs.

The base game of Castle Dice featured truly original gameplay. More Castles expands on the themes from the basic game while making sure the game still remains easy-to-learn for new players.

4 Unique Castle Decks

The big addition in More Castles, is that now each player will be using a unique Castle deck instead of sharing a common one. Each deck comes with unique new castle parts and is themed to a particular strategy/play style. This shakes up your buying decisions and can create some really fun moments.

New Castle Building Cards

We’ve opened up the design space as to what Castle “Building” cards do. More Castles introduces Castle pieces that allow you to draw more cards, ones that change how your villagers work, and a whole slew of other new abilities.

New Castle Deck Action Cards

It’s not just the castle Buildings that are new in these decks, each deck features a unique set of action cards. Cards like The Librarian who helps you get exactly the Village cards you want, the animal wrangling Stable Master, and The Puppeteer who creates a massive amount of resources on his own.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
Cursed! is a card game where fortune favors the cruel. Gather a few friends ’round the table and see how quickly all those years of bonding and camaraderie can be ripped to shreds with a well placed hex. Strategically protect your own good fortunes (Wealth, Health, Love, Fate, and Luck) while devastating the fortunes of those around you.

Each player in Cursed! is dealt a hand of three cards (blessings, curses, or neutrals each in one of five fortunes) and must choose to play one card on herself, one card on an opponent, and sacrifice one card to make a slight alteration to the rules of the game. When a player accumulates curses on three or more fortunes, he is out of the game (play continues until one player is left). If he accumulates blessings on three or more fortunes, he wins.

The game consists of 104 poker-sized cards in a tuck box and an optional player mat. There is also a print-and-play version.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

Objective: be the first reaching space 90 
 - moving by turns -

note:
likely published early 1920's

"Without a doubt, the retro beat-em-up theme and the chosen game mechanics are a match made in radioactive heaven. -Buns, The League of Nonsensical Gamers"

N30N City RUMBLE (NCR) is a two-player "beat 'em up" card game that simulates 5-on-5 tag team combat between mutant martial artists in a radically dystopian 90s setting!

Each turn, you’ll trade for cards at a pawn shop, tag your fighters on and off the tarmac, and then engage the opponent using martial arts, super-moves, and a wide variety of items, all of which are inspired by classic arcade games. Knock out all of your opponent’s fighters until only yours remain to win the game!

Features:

38 extraordinary Fighters based on popular videogames from the 90s
    44 interchangeable Brawl cards straight out of a fighting arcade game
    54 unique items and weapons that belong on the street... of rage
    5 additional game modes, including solo play for practicing moves
    12 dice to throw across a sprawling 22x22 inch playmat

The booklet Quest: Zeit der Helden - Abenteuerband 7 contains a two-player adventure for Quest


    "Stille Nacht, untote Nacht", translates to "Silent Night, Undead Night"


Additionally, all required additional material is provided, such as:

     "Questmeisterbogen"
     item and character cards
     character screen for the paladin
     mini figurine for the paladin

Quantum Mechanic is a game of combining Quarks into elementary particles, and smashing those particles together for points.
     QUANTUM MECHANIC RULES SUMMARY:

Setup the game by shuffling the actions deck, which represents actions that you may take in the game and currency that is used to bid on projects and to buy quarks from the commons. Deal six action cards to each player. then shuffle the Projects deck and deal the first three projects to the market. these are the hadrons that players will try to complete throughout the course of the game. Players then take turns bidding on projects that they want to complete, drawing quarks from a bag of plastic colored quark cubes, and smashing those particles together for points. the first player to get to nine points is the winner.

The game consists of a deck of 54 cards. On each card is a different dog breed, belonging to one of 10 different groups and one of 22 different subgroups. Each player gets five cards from the start. Each round you get to ask one player for a certain cards that you need to complete a subgroup. If the other player has the card, you get it and can ask for another card. If the other player doesn't have the requested card, you have to take a card from the draw pile and it's the next player's turn. When you have a complete subgroup, you put it on the table. When the deck runs out, you count the score. You get points for all cards from a complete subgroup, and a bonus if you have two or more subgroups within the same group.

Objective: be the first reaching space 50/1
- moving by turns -
Choose one of the two routes (red or black: Panama - Colon or vice versa): Roll the die and move forward. If you land on one of the special  marked spaces, you must obey the instructions glued into the lid.

Note:
Likely published ~1915 (not earlier than 1914/not later than early 1917 - Germany at war with USA)

BlockYou! is an energetic, fun and quick-to-play card game for 1-5 players.

You need to place your colored, blocked, square cards on the playing field faster than everyone else. Twist your cards and place them on the playing field matching all colors. Block your opponents with the pointing fingers that are printed on the cards. Try not to block yourself! The first player who places his 12 cards wins the game.

The game consists of 88 unique colored cards and rules in English. The box is easy to take along and fits into the palm of your hand.

Objective: be the first reaching space 100
- moving by turns -
Roll the die and move forward. If you land on one of the special (large) spaces follow instructions printed on the board.

Note:
Likely published early 1910's

In this micro game, there are three types of cards: Bunny, Money, and Gunny. Players secretly select cards that allow them to put their meeples into lines next to the matching cards in the play area, and to rearrange the order of the cards in the play area. The first two meeples in line then get to hop to different lines (Bunny), or eliminate opposing meeples (Gunny), or score points (Money). The first player to score 5 points wins.

KLIC Bowling is played on a plastic board reminiscent of a bowling alley, with a bowling lane for throwing two-six sided dice, and ten holes arranged like bowling pins, labeled with numbers one through ten and with plastic pegs that fit inside.  A score pad is also provided.

On each turn a player rolls the dice and removes pegs based on the outcome, then tries to remove as many more pegs as possible by jumping as in Checkers or Solitaire puzzles.  When no more moves are possible, the score is tallied exactly as in real bowling games.  A second roll of the dice is then allowed, to attempt to remove any pegs remaining.  The next player then resets the pegs and takes his turn.

Rolling a double on the first dice throw is a strike, rolling a double on the second throw is a spare.  Players track their scores in a regular bowling score format, with the winner being the player scoring the highest number of points in ten frames.
A subsidiary card set for use with the master game. Contains 1000 cards, 6000 questions in six categories: Lands and Men, Shows, Past and Present, Arts and Letters, Sciences & Nature, Sports & Games.
A subsidiary card set for use with the master game. Contains 500 cards, 3000 questions in six categories: Lands and Men, Shows, Past and Present, Arts and Letters, Sciences & Nature, Sports & Games.
family: Versions & Editions: Junior Versions of Grown-Up Games

description: A subsidiary card set for use with the master game. Contains 400 cards, 2400 questions in six categories: Places in Europe, Life in Europe, European Affairs, Culture of Europe, European cousins, Champions of Europe.

Horrible Hex is a game of strategic spatial relations, as deeply complex as a Chess match yet as simple as a game of Dominos.

There are only two components: a deck of goal cards and a set of hex tiles. There is no board; instead, the hexes form a constantly shifting maze of possible moves, blocks, and scoring opportunities.

Each player places tiles and attempts to favorably arrange the hexes to complete their goal cards and thwart their opponent’s plans.

It only takes a few minutes to learn, but a lifetime to master.

The core game is a 2-player head-to-head but rules variants allow for 3-player or solo play.

Making words from letters drawn at random is common to many word games!  But add a little cunning, an element of chance, some audacity and a lot of strategy and you get Kirdy, a word game like no other word game!  You can challenge your opponents, and take their points by gambling on your wits!  Beware though, as the sand falls ever too quickly through the egg-timer, the pressure builds!

The game starts with two letters and becomes progressively more difficult as new letters are added.  Drawing an extra letter tile after each word, the player has to find a new word, each time betting before the draw that he will be able to do so. The word has to contain the letters drawn, in the right order.

The Lion and the Mouse is a spin-and-move game for two or four players. Each player has a Lion, a Mouse and a Hunter piece. The object of the four player game is to be the first to move one's Lion and Mouse pieces from their color's Start space to the corresponding Lion's Den space. In the two player game, each player controls two colors and must move both of their Lion and Mouse pieces to the appropriate dens.

Each piece has unique traits: the Hunter can "trap" (lock down) a Mouse or send a Lion back to its Start space; the Mouse can free a Lion from a trap; the Lion can send a Hunter back to its start space from outside a trap space. The spinner determines which piece may move, though some spinner spaces permit the player to choose.

Tricky Chess is a partnership trick-taking chess game for The King's Caste deck / game system.  Through successive rounds comprising recruitment, deployment, and mobilization, each team tries to eliminate the other's two kings.

Recruitment is done through trick-taking. The card that wins the trick goes to the respective player's deployment zone, regardless of who won the trick!

After the hand is played out, players Deploy their units to the board.

Next, the teams play chess against one another with the units available, until four units are eliminated, at which point a new round starts, after a little preparation for the new round.

Objective: Be the first reaching space 50 precisely
 - moving by turns -
each player starts with 3 meeples on three different routes (tea, coffee, cacao) trying to reach space 25 (production unit)  precisely.

Ending on a marked space follow instructions. 
Successfully finished you have to move your big meeple up to space 50 (consumer).

Note:
Likely published 1960's; large folded map was sent out in a mailer.

boardgame: Heroes of Normandie: Lord Lovat's Commandos
description:
This expansion consists of an additional punchboard, that provides the player with more (and different) units choices.

Additional Scottish army counters.

Basic game is needed to play with this expansion.
family: Series: Heroes System Tactical Scale World War 2

boardgame: Second World War at Sea: The Habsburg Fleet
description:
From the Avalanche Press website:

After just over two years of terrible warfare, the Great War came to an end with the Christmas Armistice of 1916, thanks to the mediation of American President Woodrow Wilson.  But after some twenty years of peace, in 1940 the storm clouds gather once more.

The Habsburg Fleet is the second book in our alternative history series, The Second Great War.  In the first book, The Kaiser’s Navy, we looked at the High Seas Fleet of 1940 and its campaigns in the North Sea and Atlantic. In this new book, the Imperial and Royal Austro-Hungarian Navy, alongside its Turkish allies, wages war in the Mediterranean Sea.

There are 210 die-cut and mounted playing pieces from the old, out-of-print Imperial & Royal Navy booklet, and 75 brand-new laser-cut pieces (scorchless and sootless!) for 285 total: 135 double-sized "long" ship pieces and 150 standard-sized ones representing task forces, aircraft and smaller warships.  The Austro-Hungarian fleet that never was is present, along with French, British, Greek, Italian, German and Turkish ships, aircraft and airships.

There are also thirty new scenarios based on the battles that might have been waged on the Mediterranean Sea in this alternate world, plus more of the story of the Second Great War first begun in The Kaiser's Navy.  This module is not playable by itself, but requires ownership of Bomb Alley and Black Sea Fleets to enjoy all of the scenarios.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet
family: Series: Second World War at Sea (Avalanche Press)

description:
The object of the game is to have fun and learn more about the past and present of America's birthplace. A secondary object is to "win" the game by either controlling the entire city and forcing the other players out of the game or possess the most cash value at the end of an agreed on time period - 60 minutes, 90 minutes or whatever.

To start the game everyone begins at Independence Hall and receives $1776.00. Players take turns in order moving the allotted number of spaces (dice roll) in a clockwise direction around the board. If a player lands on a property with special instructions, they must be followed. If the player lands on an unowned property, it may be purchased. If the player lands on a property owned by another player, then rent must be collected. If the player lands on a property they already own, that player than receives rent from the bank. If a player rolls doubles, they roll again. A second roll of doubles send that player to the Roundhouse.

A player may sell any properties they own to any other player at any time during the game. The bank is forces to buy back any property a player choose at 67% of the original cost. The rent of each property is 10% of its original cost. If a players owns two properties of a kind (for example the Art Museum and the Academy of Music - cultural institutions) the rent is doubled. If a player owns three properties the rent is tripled. A player may not collect any rents while his pawn is at the Roundhouse or on the Expressway.

Whenever a player rolls a "one" that player gets to draw a Travel card. Whenever a player rolls a "six" that player gets to draw a News card. If a player rolls both a "one" and a "six" on the same roll that player can select either type of card, but not both. If double "ones" or "sixes" are rolled, that player draws nothing. When a player ends their turn on a block marked "Travel Card" or "News Card" that player draws the appropriate card. A player that ends their turn on either the Free Library or Franklin Institute gets to answer a Quiz card drawn by the player on their left. The "correct" answers to the Quiz cards are left to the discretion of the game players.

This mini-expansion was a non-exclusive stretch goal during the Kickstarter campaign of the Tortuga base game and will be available as promo. It adds 17 alternative treasure tiles.

Playable only with the Tortuga base game.

boardgame: Trivial Pursuit: TV Edition – Card Set
description:
Card set for use with the Trivial Pursuit: TV Edition – Master Game.


Each player has a circular playing piece with six pie-shaped holes. The goal of the game is to collect a pie in each color. The colors correspond to different question categories.

The board consists of a circular track with spaces in seven different colors. Six of the colors correspond to question categories while the last color gives a new dice roll. Six spaces along the track are "pie spaces", and from these there are "spokes" of track leading to the middle of the board.

Players roll a die and move along the track in any direction they like. When a player stops on a color they get a question of the appropriate category. If the player answers a question correctly while on a pie space, they get a pie of that color (assuming they don't already have it). A correct answer on another square allows the player to roll again.

Once the player has one pie in each color, she can move along the spokes to the middle of the board to win the game.

Released by the end of 2011, not long after the 2011 Standard edition, it contains 2011 Standard edition plus all official expansions. Its releasing date was so close to the end of the year that it hit some local markets only early next year, so it was sometimes mistaken as 2012 Complete edition.

Legends of the Three Kingdoms (a.k.a. San Guo Sha) is a card game based off the Chinese classical novel, Romance of the Three Kingdoms, which shares many rules with Bang!, the differences are as follows:
1. The Monarch (Sheriff) may get an additional skill related to their kingdom allegiance; players routinely have two powers.
2. Weapons may have their own additional powers.
3. A card to let a player borrow a weapon from another.
4. A card to cancel the effect of other cards.
5. Peach (Beer) may be played by any player when someone loses their last life; there is no exemption of using Peach when there are only 2 players left.
6. The game proceeds counter-clockwise.
family: Theme: Romance of the Three Kingdoms

description: This is a car race, so obviously the winner is the one who is the first to reach the finish line. The movement of the racing cars is determined by a die. Several segments of the race track have special rules, like the results of the die counting double or half. The Players get two different kinds of cards they may use during the race instead of rolling the die. The red spurt cards allow the car to advance from a space with a yellow arrow to the next space with a red tableau. The green attack cards allow a car to exchange it's positon with any one opponent on the throw of an even number. But beware: If the die shows an odd number, the car remains on it's position and doesn't advance at all.

boardgame: San Guo Sha: Guan Yu Stamp Edition
description:
UNAUTHORIZED PRODUCTION

    
    
         The information that BGG has on this item indicates that San Guo Sha is an unauthorized reproduction of BANG! with just a few differences, published in China and distributed in several coutries of the world.

    


It's a limited edition (20000 copies with unique numbers) for collectors. It contains the base game plus all official expansions (Battle, Wind, Fire, Woods and Mountain).

In the 2011 Guan Yu Stamp edition, two characters were changed:

Zhou Yu (WU, from base game): the second ability now gives out the card before the damage is dealt.

Zhu Rong (SHU, from Fire exp.): the second ability allows a point duel only with the direct victim of her Strike (not with shackled players).


Legends of the Three Kingdoms (a.k.a. San Guo Sha) is a card game based off the Chinese classical novel, Romance of the Three Kingdoms, which shares many rules with Bang!, the differences are as follows:
1. The Monarch (Sheriff) may get an additional skill related to their kingdom allegiance; players routinely have two powers.
2. Weapons may have their own additional powers.
3. A card to let a player borrow a weapon from another.
4. A card to cancel the effect of other cards.
5. Peach (Beer) may be played by any player when someone loses their last life; there is no exemption of using Peach when there are only 2 players left.
6. The game proceeds counter-clockwise.
family: Theme: Romance of the Three Kingdoms

boardgame: San Guo Sha: The Return of Legend
description:
UNAUTHORIZED PRODUCTION

    
    
         The information that BGG has on this item indicates that San Guo Sha is an unauthorized reproduction of BANG! with just a few differences, published in China and distributed in several coutries of the world.

    


All official expansions (Battle, Wind, Fire, Woods and Mountains) released in 2011, at the same time with 2011 Standard edition of the base game.

Yoka released this edition together with the 2011 Standard edition, updating the terminology and the new cardback so that it can be mixed with the latter.

description:
The beginner's edition (初出茅庐) released in 2008.

Targeted to simplify teaching, it contains only 64 cards in deck instead of 108 in a standard one, 4 heroes (Guan Yu, Gan Ning, tweaked Lv Bu and tweaked Zhang Liao) instead of 25.

It has no box, just a big envelope with a page of rule sheet and 4 sheets of uncut cards inside. You need to cut out the cards by yourself.
