description:
Players take the role of a god in a friendly competition to reach the Glory goal set by the crowd, directing their champion in a wide array of arena-style games: Classics like King of the Hill, new modes like Shrine of the Wind, all played out via a simple rock-paper scissors mechanic with direct conflict resolved by a simple die roll without player elimination and the arenas sorted into easy-to-grasp zones.

The game features three different modes: Basic, designed for new or young players and quicker, simpler gameplay; Advanced, a more strategic mode where each player truly takes on the role of a God with resource management and asymmetric powers to twist the fates of their pawns in the arena; and Intermediate, which introduces the Advanced mode concepts and the active role of the player as one of the all-new pantheon of Gods in the Cauldron!

As part of the mission of Civilized Animal Productions to emphasize the role of games not just as a way to build relationships, relax, and have fun but also to teach the young, Cauldron of the Gods offers plenty of opportunity to discuss history and culture in the form of the Gods, emphasize sportsmanship by eliminating the other players as 'enemies' by using a system of proxy conflict and painting the game as a competition, and help curb cheating or disengagement by using a directly personal approach to conflicts between players and adopting a story-like, cinematic expression of the actual fighting. The game has frequent moments of tension between peaceable moments, which can be used to defuse negative behaviors and infuse positive approaches without interrupting the flow of the game. The Advanced and Intermediate modes of play are more complicated and this may be daunting to the younger range of players.

This game requires the following components from TKG ARENA: The Elemental Kings and is a standalone extension within the TKG ARENA family.

STANDARD DECK (DECK):    1

The Elemental Cycle

The concept of the elemental cycle is introduced into the game. This refers to the different relationships each element has with the others according to their dominance, opposition and weakness.

Fire (Ashw) dominates over Earth (Ohif), opposes Air (Ukelele) and is weak against Water (Esbner).

Water (Esbner) dominates over Fire (Ashw), opposes Earth (Ohif) and is weak against Air (Ukelele).

Air (Ukelele) dominates over Water (Esbner), opposes Fire (Ashw) and is weak against Earth (Ohif).

Earth (Ohif) dominates over Air (Ukelele), opposes Water (Esbner) and is weak against Fire (Ashw).

The objective of this game scenario is to be the first to discard all your cards in your CURRENT HAND. The recommended numbers playing the game will be 2, 3, 4 and 6 who will be distributed with 18, 12, 9 and 6 cards respectively from a shuffled STANDARD DECK.

Players start the game by each drawing a card from the top of a common shuffled DECK. The player with the highest number starts first and the players get their turns in a clockwise manner. The drawn cards go back to the bottom of the DECK. In the case of ties, the tied players draw again till the turns are resolved. The starting player may lay down any 1 card from his/her hand into a common pool in front of all players.

During each turn, each player may EITHER 1) play a card whose FACE VALUE is higher than the previous card; 2) play a card whose ELEMENT dominates the ELEMENT of the previous card; 3) pass and the turn goes to the next player.

Each time a player plays a card whose ELEMENT dominates the ELEMENT of the previous card, each player MUST choose a card from his/her CURRENT HAND and pass it to the player on the left.

In the situation when the game cannot go on as players can no longer put down cards due to the rules, the player with the least cards of the smallest number wins.

A doujinshi (self-published) simulation of the Battle of Dannoura in 1185 (the sea battle that brought the Genpei War in Japan to a close), Shogyoumujou (諸行無常) is a Buddhist concept translated as “the impermanence of all things”. This is the main theme of the “Heike Monogatari”, a gunkimono (war tale) that gives a highly romanticized account of the war and the times that surrounded it. The book details the rise and fall of the Taira warrior house, the losing side at Dannoura. It drives home the point that “all that flourishes must fall” and that in a world of impermanence even the mighty are eventually reduced to “dust before the wind”. It’s a very fitting title, as the battle effectively ended the Taira's reign as a political and military powerhouse.

It has several sub-titles that change according to the game component-the full name is Shogyoumujou: Genpei Arasoi- Setouchi No Koubou-Genpei Saigo No Kassen-Dannoura No Tatakai (諸行無常: 源平の争い-瀬戸内の攻防-源平最後の合戦-壇ノ浦の戦い). This mouthful of a name translates as “Impermanence Of All Things: Genpei Strife-Offense And Defense Of The Seto Inland Sea-End of the Genpei War-Battle of Dannoura.

The game features the naval forces of the Taira and Minamoto warrior houses and their assorted allies. In a strange design decision, the historical situation is somewhat reversed-the Minamoto are severely outnumbered but have stronger units than their Taira foes (the Minamoto substantially outnumbered the Taira but the crews of the Taira ships were far more experienced at sea warfare as the clan first came to power by demonstrating their prowess at fighting pirates). Perhaps this was done to reflect the Minamoto’s lack of seaborne experience (the reduced numbers) but to show their large advantage in tactics (the increased strength)-their commander, Minamoto no Yoshitsune, purportedly ordered his archers to fire upon the rowers of the boats rather than the enemy soldiers (a violation of the unwritten rules of warfare of the day, something that Yoshitsune was notorious for throughout his career). While the game is relatively simple, it has lots of chrome rules that add historical flavor such as the effects of the changing tide on movement and combat, possession of the Imperial Regalia and the Child Emperor Antoku (both initially held by the Taira), defections, the role of leaders, and giving the Taira a couple of unique ship types. Historically, the Minamoto literally used the ‘turning of the tide’ to rout the Taira and were aided by key defections during the battle as well as Yoshitsune’s less than ‘honorable’ tactics. The Taira leader, Munemori, was fished out of the ocean (where he had been thrown by an ally disgusted at his reluctance to commit suicide to avoid capture and dishonor) and eventually executed. However, the Taira denied the Minamoto a complete victory when the matriarch of the clan and her attendants jumped into the ocean with the child Emperor and the Imperial Regalia. While the Minamoto managed to recover two of the three artifacts, the child Emperor died and the sword Kusanagi was lost forever. This was a major disappointment to the Minamoto’s alleged supporter, Retired Emperor Go-Shirakawa. The game’s victory conditions reflect all this, factoring both the Emperor/Regalia and enemy losses into the mix. It’s a fast (10 turns, a couple of hours) and fun play with a manageable amount of units and good balance.

boardgame: HYM: The Return of the Heroes
description:
Heroes and Mercenaries the return of the heroes is a fantasy-themed eurogame for 1-4 players getting all the feelings that fans of RPGs love with almost no random factor.

Each player gets into the skin of a farmer who must become a hero to the force, evil lurks around every corner, closer to their homes and farms.

Fight bravely, and may keep the skin and perhaps win gold and fame. But remember, not everything is black or white, and no one will look bad if you hire mercenaries to help in the dirtiest and / or difficult tasks.

Explore temples and swipe artifacts with your friends! You won't get very far alone, but who knows if your friends can be trusted?

In Ruinous, each player is an adventurer attempting to collect valuable items within dangerous ruins. They must brave these deadly ruins while balancing teamwork and trickery on the way to finding ancient Artifacts - powerful and expensive items that imbue their holders with unique abilities. But be careful: no item or player is safe from treachery! The first player to collect and sell at least $1000 worth of treasure and artifacts wins!

When it's your turn to explore a ruin you may first assemble an adventure party made up of other players in the game. The more people you recruit, the easier it is to overcome traps and obstacles. But keep in mind, they're still your enemies! Offer them a cut of the treasure to encourage cooperation, but remember: in the depths of the world's darkest ruins there are no contracts, there are no witnesses and sometimes only one survivor... So always prepare for an opportunistic backstabbing.

Should you enlist the help of your rivals or attempt to go it alone? Are your fellow adventurers trustworthy? Are you?

Forest Sprite is a cooperative game for 1-3 players in which players control a team of sprites and forest creatures on a mission to defend their home from an invading force of evil sorcerers and the monsters and misfortune they bring into your forest.

Forest Sprite can be played as a standalone board game (challenge mode) in which player(s) can choose pre-generated character(s) and play any 1 of the Chapters. HOWEVER
Sprite is best enjoyed by playing in campaign mode. In campaign mode, a player creates his or her own character and uses that character to attempt to beat each Chapter in order. Chapters get progressively more challenging, but as your character gains experience, it may attain new levels. When your character attains a new level, experience points can be spent to add new spells and abilities and upgrade stats. Characters will also pick up items as they progress through Chapters and defeat monsters.

Gameplay is pretty simple. The turn begins with the drawing of a mission and moving of any monsters that are on the board. The player may then take 3 actions using his or her character and/or forest animals in order to complete one of the open missions. When players fail missions, monsters get released. If 6 missions are failed, player loses. If the players draw all of the missions and fail less than 6 of them, they win (and gain experience points in campaign mode).

Forest Sprite is a game full of tense decisions. (starting with creating a good character!) There are no dice (the only luck is in the order in which missions are drawn). This is a game of planning, skillful gameplay, and coordination of resources.

Dream City is a multiplayer strategy game. Players will become residents and go through the different historical events of the Hong Kong community.

When facing the reconstruction and demolition of the community, players will decide how to develop the "ideal community." To become a winner, you have to ponder how to preserve valuable cultural environment, taking into account the affected stakeholders, including vulnerable groups who are often neglected in the mainstream economy.

Through the game, players can easily recognized community development, to explore issues of poverty, social development can rethink how the interests of justice. Game in conjunction with the school curriculum (Liberal Studies, Life and Social Studies), leadership training activities, life education topics, moral and civic education and career planning, etc., It is recommended to teachers, social workers, parents and other educators.

《你想社區Dream City》是一款多人策略版圖遊戲教材。玩家成為居民，經歷香港社區發生的大小事件，面對重建、清拆，玩家將決定如何發展「理想社區」。要成為真正的大贏家，你必須深思如何保留有價值的文化和事物，同時兼顧受影響的持分者，包括往往在主流經濟發展下備受忽略的弱勢社群。

透過遊戲，玩家可以足不出戶，輕輕鬆鬆認識社區發展，探討貧窮議題，重新思考社會發展如何能合乎公義。遊戲同時配合學校課程(通識科、生活與社會科)、領袖培訓活動、生命教育課題、德育及公民教育與生涯規劃等，是老師、社工、家長及其他教育工作者的好幫手。

description: Monster Deck 55 is card deck system. It has 55 unique monsters on 18 different colored backgrounds. 3 different suits (airplanes, arrows and angles) 1-18 in each suit. Plus one joker. This deck can be used to play many different games.

Boardcraft is a modular board building and manipulation game. Set in an eclectic fantasy world, players build a board during the game, but can also change the board throughout the game. The board is consisted of 3x3 tiles with a random assortment of different terrains on them, including grass, water, marsh, boulder and lava.

Each player can also change the board in unique ways by swapping tiles, removing tiles or rotating tiles. This is most important because there are lava squares and players always need to be aware of how those tiles can change and move so they don't end up in lava and die.

There are also items to take including Weapons, Armor, Potions and Boardcrafting cards. On a turn, a player can make an attack, pick up an item, as well as play a "Genesis action" which means they can add a tile to the board.

The goal is to collect achievement points by completely missions and killing other players. Missions are randomly distributed and might be anything from collecting items, to performing in game actions such as pushing someone into water.

Players can play in teams or free-for-all in completely new environments each game.

Players take role of Dwarfs running away from dragon's lair, after stealing it's treasures.

The goal of the game is to give as much of the gold you carry to other players, so they will run slower. In the end, only one dwarf can escape the lair, others will be devoured or burned by the raging dragon.

The game uses a simple take that card mechanic, with cards that makes other player take some of your gold, give some of the gold of all players to one dwarf, or make the dwarf take some gold from the common pile.

Manage a football club and not let go until the last detail. Manage your players transfers, hire a coach, tactics, lineups, youth team, club stores, expand the stadium, shareholders, reservations, training, finance, sports betting...

The goal is not to win the league, the goal is to be the player with the most manger points at the end of the game although naturally win titles helps to get points. Get points with reforms manager, hiring employees ...

description:
Blood Rush is a short and thematic card-based micro-game designed for solo play about a group of monsters raiding a small town. The goal of the player is to capture seven victims before the Inquisition arrives to the town.

Each round, four Villager cards are set up face-down, guarding the four different Town Locations, and an Event die is rolled to determine how the round will play. Then, the player reveals the first Villager and assigns the Monster cards, either to the Town Locations or to the Lair, a Special location that allows the player to manipulate luck.

The player must balance his or her forces in an effort to capture the required victims and avoid the nasty effects of the defending villagers. Positioning the Monsters and making clever use of their abilities will be essential for success!

In Flocks & Flyways, the goal is to collect the most Migration Tokens by creating and migrating Flocks of Birds south for the winter to warmer feeding grounds. Players will need to strategically navigate crowded Flyways and catch Strong Tailwinds while avoiding dangerous Predators, Fog and other hazards. The player with the most Migration Tokens when there are no tokens remaining wins the game.

Gameplay Overview:

Each turn is comprised of three phases:

1) Draft Cards and Play Complete Flocks:


    Each player may draft either two Birds or one Action from the Feeding Grounds. 



    If a player is able to play a complete Flock(s), it may be played in any vacant, valid Flyway for that Bird type. A player may play as many complete Flocks from her hand during her turn. A player is not required to play a full Flock even if there is an open Flyway. 
            For example, a player may want to hold onto a complete Flock to create an even larger Flock for more points.
        
    



    After drafting cards and playing any Flocks, that player refills the Feeding Grounds back up to six cards to signal her turn is complete. If the draw pile is empty, reshuffle the discard pile to form a new draw pile.



2) Play Actions: After each player has had the opportunity to draft and play Flocks, beginning with the person with the player one token, each player may play one Action from her hand.

3) Migrate: Starting with Pacific Flyway, moving in order towards the Eastern Flyway, place one Migration Token on each Flock currently in play, unless prohibited by an Action (i.e. Fog).


    If the number of tokens on the Flock is equal to the Migration Time printed on the card, the Flock owner collects all Migration Tokens currently on the Flock and the Flock is then discarded. Full Flock Bonus: The owner of the Flock receives +1 additional Migration Token from the pile for each additional Bird over the required Flock size. Safety in numbers!


After the Migrate phase is complete, all players must discard down to the hand limit of ten cards. The player one token is then passed clockwise, shifting the starting player for the new turn. Refill the Feeding Grounds and continue play until all Migration Points are expended.

Winning the Game

    When the last token is played on a Flock, the game ends immediately. Any Migration Tokens on Flocks in a Flyway go to the owner of those Flocks. Each player adds up their token and the player with the most points wins!

Objective: be the first to get the permanent majority of votes for the coming election of the mayor - learn more about the city of Fürth
- moving/acting by turns.

Each player rolls the die, moves according the number, follows instructions of space landed on; buys, if possible and/or (preferable) votes.

"Chance" cards are created with circumstances out of the history of Detmold. Correct answers give economic advantages.

Voting process about the next mayor: Each owned company corresponds to a number of votes. The results of each voting are kept and the final winner is the first player who reaches the target.

"Who knows Kaiserslautern - The Original City Quiz"

Game last until there are no players or the session is over.

Player with the most points is the winner.

description:
StartUp is a business board game in which players compete to guide their startup company from launch to the completion of a corporate headquarters. Along the way there are Business Opportunities, Capital Opportunities, and IPO Opportunities. Build Expertise to increase your chances for success and your company's value in an Initial Public Offering. Navigate lawsuits, price wars, networking opportunities and shock cards along the way.
Unlike other business board games, Startup is a lot of fun, even when you are not winning. You lose by succeeding more slowly, not by getting crushed.

The Startup board game teaches business and math skills and models many real business situations, such as the value of investing in your business, the risks and rewards of business opportunities, and the trade-offs in a Capital Opportunity or an IPO.

Startup is fun for the whole family and will have your 9 year old jumping up and down thrilled to have completed an IPO!

StartUp is challenging for any age from child to adult. Although there is some luck involved, the better player will usually win, kind of like real life.

Che noia, essere un invicibile drago. Nessuno osa più entrare nella stanza del tesoro, e non basta il luccichio delle monete per divertirsi. Cosi il drago decide di lanciare la sfida: lascerà passare il migliore. Ed ecco il tortuoso sotterraneo riempirsi di eroi, principesse, maghi, nani, impostori e presenze invisibili. Ma occorre fare presto, prima che arrivi il suo tremendo sbadiglio!

How boring it is to be an invincible dragon. No one dares to enter the treasure room, and not just the glint of coins to have fun. So the dragon decides to launch the challenge: I will let pass the best! From that moment the tortuous underground filled with heroes, princesses, wizards, dwarves, thieves and invisible presences. 
But they must act quickly, before they gets its tremendous... YAWN!

All the most beautiful Princesses have been invited to the Princess School of Ballet to learn how to dance one of the most famous Princess ballets. The Princess to learn and perform her ballet first is crowned the winner.

Each player must find and learn all 5 of their ballet dance poses that are shown – on the smaller pose cards – on their ballet card. These poses are found on the giant dance floor playing mat. When a correct matching pose is found, the Princess must perform that pose and then place the card face down on her ballet card. Being able to remember all the poses in the correct order is the challenge.

On each princesses turn, they must roll the foam dice to see what step they must take, will it be; a Footstep (move either 1, 2 or 3 steps depending on the amount indicated on the dice), a Lesson Symbol (a private dance lesson where you are allowed to peek at either 1 or 2 of the game cards that are laid face down) or the Shuffle Symbol(where you can swap 3 poses on the board to either help you or delay your opponents dance).

If you are the Princess who has collected all 5 of their ballet poses first, then you can perform your dance first to see if you have won. Turn your pose cards over and have a look – now hand the card to the other Princesses and recite the ballet dance in the order same order that the poses are shown on the card. Got them right? Then you are the winner!

Suitable for 2 – 4 players.

The players have to try to form words on the game board. For this they can freely choose from the 344 letters in the game. Each turn, each player may add only 1 letter. If a player completes a word, he gets Points awarded, dependent on the lenght of the Word.

This mini-expansion consists of four cards, a double-sided "Attic" card for each player.

Eight extra installation cards to add to your Cubist experience.  This mini expansion includes:

Wargames Rules for the Wars of the Late 17th & Early 18th Centuries

The period 1650 to 1712 saw dramatic changes in the conduct of European warfare with the final demise of the pike and the introduction of first the plug-bayonet and then its far handier
successor the socket-bayonet. The heavy cavalry shed most of its armour and began to rely more and more on shock action. The infantry changed over from firing volleys by ranks and adopted the far more flexible platoon firing. Light cavalry began to make its appearance on the battlefield.

Command and control was no less rudimentary. Marlborough’s running footmen, on hand to carry his orders to all parts of the field, were the wonder of the day. A general staff, trained to act as a team, was still years in the future.
And as for the name, Corporal John was the affectionate nickname given to John, first Duke of Marlborough by his troops.
These rules are designed to replicate the warfare of Marlborough and Eugene, of Charles XII and Peter the Great, and of the great Condé and Turrenne. They might easily be used for the conflicts of Cromwell and Prince Rupert or even the wars of Maurice de Saxe. Whichever you choose.
All models fielded under these rules are organised on “element” bases. They can be of any size provided that both sides field their forces on the same size bases. There is no figure-scale as such – the gamer is free to use as many or as few figures as he wishes and the base will accommodate – and no removal of casualties.

Naval Warfare in the Age of the Dreadnought.

These rules are designed for naval wargames in the era from the introduction of the turreted battleship until air power took over from surface action as the dominate factor in naval war - this includes the Russo-Japanese War of 1905, WWI and the early part of WWII.

These rules are primarily designed for games where each player commands a squadron of about 4 capital ships & maybe a squadron of light cruisers & a flotilla or two of DD’s. Such games with 1 or 2 players a side can be played comfortably in an evening.

Smaller actions with a ship per player (like the Hood v. Bismark) are good for a quick fun game or as training for beginners.

It is designed for multiplayer games with each player commanding either a single ship or a squadron of a few ships. The principles of the game are:
1. The game is played on a hex grid. The scale of the ship models doesn’t matter as long as they fit in a hex.
2. Each ship has a ship’s log to record proposed manoeuvres and damage. These are magnetic boards with paper overlays with details recorded by positioning magnetic counters.
3. The players secretly record their proposed movement each turn.
4. All the ships are then moved simultaneously in accordance with those proposals.
5. Firing of main guns, secondary armament & torpedoes is then resolved ship by ship.

Rules for fighting Divisional level battles from the Spanish Civil War, using 15 mm figures.

The game is played on a grid of offset squares or hexagons. These represent an area 500 yards across. For 15mm figures on 30mm bases use xx squares.

Each area will normally hold a battalion sized unit, plus a support arm, such as an armour section or an artillery battery. A square can, however, contain two battalions if wanted. Squares may hold a maximum, of one artillery battery or tank squadron (unless parked up in reserve)

Note that all the units in a square are subject to the effect of the total hits on the square when casualties are determined.

An FBI Hostage Rescue Unit game

The point of this game is to rescue hostages and kill terrorists with automatic weapons. One side is the Terrorists, and the other side is the FBI’s Hostage Rescue unit. Each person can control up to 3 people in a fire team, and they use the same map to control them in the Action phase. The map can be anything from a blueprint to an actual model. The objective of the Terrorists is to get their demands met, or kill the FBI Hostage Rescue guys, forcing the FBI to back down. The point for the FBI is to free the hostages. The hostages follow the team.

International Terrorist Suppression Agency

In this game, you take control of either a force of INTSA Operatives, deployed covertly to infiltrate and destroy from within; a force of armed police and SAS to overtly break sieges with military force; a band of Russian Mafiya Gangsters, who supply the Lincolnists with weapons; or a group of Lincolnist rebels intent of a terror mission only they know of.

The characters
Each character has a number of values that show how good he is. They are:
•    Movement (M) – used to make tests to jump obstacles, climb walls, etc.
•    Accuracy factor (Ac) – used to determine the effectiveness of your shooting
•    Armour factor (Ar) – used to reduce the effectiveness of enemy shooting
•    Negotiation factor (N) – used to negotiate the surrender of enemy troops
•    Technical factor (T) – used in tests to defuse bombs, hack computers, etc.
•    Language factor (L) – used to understand foreign languages
They also have a variety of equipment depending on what type of character he is (more on this in the force selection lists – see the section entitled Force Selection).

Moving
Each character can normally move six inches if models are 20mm to 30mm in size, six centimetres if models are 15mm in size, or 12” if over 30mm in size (not recommended). For measurements, these rules will refer to ‘units’. One unit equates to 1cm in 15mm scale, 1” in 20-30mm scale, and 2” in +30mm scale.

In a futuristic world where humans are established in several galaxy, a large magnetic storm hit the galaxy Virgo, destroying most of the high end technology. Hundreds of years later, scientists from ECHO, the largest planet in the galaxy Virgo, recreated rockets capable of traveling between planets at very high speed. Then, Globocorporation appealed to young spirited ECHO's heirs for conquest the galaxy Virgo!

In the game ECHO, players assume the role of entrepreneurs running a business of conquest. They will take control of certain strategic points on the board while managing their resources and keeping an eye on their opponents.

ECHO is a real time game that is played primarily in teams of 2 players (even if there are multiple additional modes to the game). Players must occupy different roles within the team. Here are the main roles found in the game ECHO:

- Resource Management Mechanism (worker placement, marketplace management and hourglasses) 
- Device Management (moving on the board mechanism and rolling dice) 
- Management of mini-games (mazes, tile placement, gambling, puzzles)


Dans une monde futuriste où l'humain est établi dans plusieurs galaxie, une grand orage magnétique a frappé la galaxie Virgo, détruisant la plupart de la technologie de pointe. Dans centaines d'années plus tard, les scientifiques d'ECHO, la plus grande planète de la galaxie Virgo, on recréé des fusées capables de voyager entre les planètes à très grande vitesse. Globocorporation lance alors un appel aux jeunes héritiers fougueux d'ECHO, reconquérir la galaxie de Virgo!

Dans le jeu ECHO, les joueurs incarnent des entrepreneurs à la tête d'une entreprise de conquête. Ils devront prendre le contrôle de certains points stratégiques sur le plateau tout en faisant la gestion de leurs ressources et en gardant l'oeil sur leurs adversaires.

ECHO est un jeu en temps réel qui se joue principalement en équipe de 2 (même s'il existe plusieurs modes de jeux supplémentaires). Les joueurs occupent doivent faire la gestion de différents rôles au sein de l'équipe. Voici la liste des rôles principaux que l'on retrouve dans le jeu ECHO :

- Gestion des ressources (mécanisme de workerplacement, marketplace et gestion des sabliers)
- Gestion des unités (mécanisme de déplacement et de roulement de dés)
- Gestion des mini-jeux (labyrinthes, placement de tuiles, jeux de hasard, casse-tête)

Take on the role of a pirate in this fun game of greed and revenge attempt to build your treasure hoard as the other players attempt to pilfer your coffers but they better watch out because you are all pirates and two can play at that game.

This is a good game for those that enjoy intense strategy but it is also simple and easy to learn so it is accessible for both children and advanced adult players alike. Above all it is fun there will be a lot of trash talking and grudges but no one will feel victimized for long because in this game its never long before the victim becomes predator and vice versa.

Rules

Object: be the first to add ten treasure cards to your hoard

Great War is Battlefront's expansion of their Flames of War rules, adapting them from WW2 to reflect combat in WW1, specifically focusing on 1918.

The rules are provided as a 37 page supplement that requires the 3rd edition core FoW rulebook in order to use. Battlefront is supporting the Great War rules with two initial opposing forces: Germans and British. These two armies will be available around the August/September 2014 timeframe and will include infantry centric forces supported by artillery and small sections of armored tanks.

In this game you have to drive from your car park, around the city and return - you have to drive as good and safe as possible, following the traffic rules!
During your drive, several things can occur and you can be asked to recognize the different traffic signs.
The winner is the one first to arrive back in the car park.

The game has changed a bit over the years and has been made in different editions:

1st Edition (1950s): Colourful board with a lot of humoristic graphics

2nd Edition (early 1960s): The game gets a name change, and the board gets a redesign; the ferry in the lower left corner is moved, the parking is moved to that spot and the board gets less colours and different graphics.

3rd edition (late 1960s): The game gets it's old name back. Not much more information is known about this edition.

4th edition (1977): Board is back to the original design with ferry, in colour and a lot of drawings again. The game is revised to follow the Traffic Code activated May 1st 1977.

5th edition (1990s): New publisher and new name. A modernized design, but still with the original idea. More rules are put into the game as well as a point system and more means of transportation.

Probably the same game as Færdselsspil.
publishers: Nordiska Papperskompaniet Ab: Oy Pohjoismaiden Paperikomppania

boardgame: Hansel und Gretel ein unterhaltendes Gesellschaftsspiel
description:
A simple roll and move course with events from the Grimm fairy tale.
 - be the first reaching space 24/moving by turns -
use the die & move forward: depending on the result/space you land on throw is going to be modified
note:
published ~1900
family: Folk Tales & Fairy Tales: Hansel and Gretel

description:
Sword Song of Edge is a 2D Dungeon Crawler for 1 to 5 players, with the goal of saving  a world plunged in the darkness by recovering the heart of the dungeons, an object of unknown usefulness divided in nine chunks and concealed in the depths of every scene.

The game is composed of nine acts. Each of them can be played idependently, so a single act forms an entertaining adventure. But, each act has been prepared and designed to connect with the other acts. Every new act expands the arsenal which our heroes have and presents the players with more difficulties during their adventure.

Game System

The scenes are represented in 2D. Players move on tiles of 10 x 7 squares, with a simple system to advance or to attack. The tiles are placed in a random manner, forming a dungeon filled with interesting locations and enemies to fight. As the players advances, new tiles are revealed, with new enemies and connections to other tiles or the exit.

When an enemy is revealed, the players will reach for their weapons in order to defeat it and continue their advance.

The goal is simple. To reach the dungeons, overcome the challenges there, find the heart of the dungeon and then reach the exit to finish the adventure.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description: 2-player game. Each player must move his boxer in the box adjacent to the opponent and hit him. The punch cardboard and electric wheel randomly selects a shot and how many points collects the attacker. To the twelfth point or exits the KO shot the attacker wins the round.

Weird is Relative ...
Creep on BOARD with the kookiest GAME around.
The aim of the Game is to be the first Addams to go round the Board avoiding the obstacles and land on the Tombstone.

A board game in the world of the webserie "The Visitor from the future".

The players must find a way to save the Earth from future cataclysms.

They begin by using cards to built and modify the future. Each player must complete his own story by playing cards from his hand on his own board : locations, characters, events, consequences... These cards are drawn each turn but can also be traded or bought to other players using "batteries" - the currency that can be earned each turn on dice rolls.

Then, the first player to have a finished story with enough victory points wins and tells the whole story to others in an improvisation tour-de-force.

Dragon Raiders is a game where players take on the role of the Dragon, raiding the fantasy kingdom for food (to grow bigger and stronger) and treasures (to gain an advantage over the other Dragons).

Game-play consists of a series of Raid phases, broken up into turns by an Accounting phase (where certain play elements get reset). Each Raid phase consists of three sub-phases; movement, reactive board elements, and combat, in that order. Players accomplish each sub-phase in an order determined by their power ranking. Players choose where to move based on what they think they can successfully raid, or may choose to go on adventures in the deepest forest. After the Dragons move, Knights will come out of their keeps to fight any Dragon nearby (i.e., adjacent to the keep).

Combat consists of rolling 1d6 and adding your Power attribute; you must beat the Defense score of the board element in order to win. A Dragon who does not win his combat must retreat, losing half of his carried treasure as he does so. Dragons must fight other Dragons in the same space first; both roll 1d6 and add their Power, highest total wins. The loser gives half of his treasure to the winner and retreats. Ties result in both dragons retreating without losing any treasure.

The end-game is triggered when a Dragon defeats the King, who sits in his castle at the center of the kingdom. The current Raid phase is finished out, and victory points are totaled. The highest total is considered the strongest Dragon and wins the game!

Players serve themselves letter tiles from a bowl with a spoon. The object of this phase is to get as many tiles as possible but players risk being accused of not being genteel and forfeiting tiles if they have more than 35.

Each player then tries to make as many words as possible from their letters within three minutes. Points are scored for the number of syllables created. There is then a three-minute partnership round in which pairs of partners make as many words as they can from their shared existing words and their pooled unused tiles. The winners are the partners with the highest combined scores from both rounds.

Simpler variants are offered for children and for solitaire play.

Welcome to Combat soldier. You must lead your troops to victory in midst of defeat.

The tactical desicions you make can send the enemy fleeing the battlefield or witness your own men dying. This game combines detailed skirmish combat designed for fast play but with enough realism and grit to make it realistic and accurate to real combat.

Scale of Game- The turn are supposed to act as if they were fought simultaneously. This is 30 seconds in the game world for a Skirmish
game, Each inch is equal to about 1 foot in the game for skirmish actions.

Organization: This game is meant to be played at a skirmish level. But we have included rules for other styles of gaming which is perfectly fine, but should stay towards platoon-company battles due to the skirmish nature of these rules.

This game can be played with any miniatures you like! They do not have to be from any specific company or manufacturer.

Wargaming rules for WW2 naval battles.

This set of rules has been developed to give a reasonably historical result for a large fleet action in about three hours of playing time

Scales and time
Each game turn is calculated to be about 15 minutes and 1000 yards equals one inch on the game table. This game scale works best with 1:3000 scale models. For larger scales such as 1:2400 or 1:1200, double all measurements. For 1:6000 halve all measurements.

These rules have been written to give an easily playable game which reflects the style of battles fought during the American War of Independence, and the French and Indian Wars. To simplify the game a standard size of unit is employed and only a few major formations are permitted. Figures are not removed but a simple record needs to be kept of the status of each unit.

These rules assume that 25mm figures will be used, but they can be used for 15mm by using 2/3 the distances specified. The scales are flexible and can be adjusted to reflect the size of battle being fought provided the ratios are maintained.

A game for battles in the horse and musket era, a period covering the 18th and 19th centuries. You can play fictional battles or try and refight famous battles from the period covered.
These rules can be used with any scale of miniature

boardgame: Star Trek: Attack Wing – Fleet Captains Resource
description: The Fleet Captain Cards are a participation prize given to players who participated in Month 2, "Battle of Wolf 359," of the Star Trek: Attack Wing Storyline Organized Play Event, The Collective. It is considered a Resource, and costs 5 Squadron Points.

boardgame: Star Trek: Attack Wing – Ti'Mur Vulcan Expansion Pack
description: This ship is the competitive prize for Month 1 of the Star Trek: Attack Wing Organized Play tournament, The Collective.

boardgame: Star Trek: Attack Wing – U.S.S. Raven Expansion Pack
description: This ship is the competitive prize for Month 2 of the Star Trek: Attack Wing Organized Play tournament, The Collective.

boardgame: Star Trek: Attack Wing – The Collective Month 2 Organized Play Borg Cube and tokens
description:
Comes with the following for use with the "Battle of Wolf 359" scenario as a part of Month 1 of "The Collective" organized play tournaments:

1 Borg Cube (ship) Token with unique stats
6 Debris Tokens
16 Damage Tokens (8 red & 8 blue)

boardgame: Run, Fight, or Die!: Expansion Pack 3
description:
From the box:

"Now you can play Cooperatively! Expansion 3 adds two decks that allow players to play together against the hordes of the Undead. Each player has a unique goal. Action Cards are available to help the players achieve those goals. Also included are 4 New Locations and 2 New Character Boards.

Included in the box:

    2 new character boards
    8 Personal Goal cards
    16 Action cards
    4 new location cards

Spot it! Match & Learn consists of two Spot it! games: Spot it! Numbers & Shapes and Spot it! Alphabet. Both games feature similar gameplay, with players trying to spot matches as quickly as possible.

Each card in Spot it! Numbers & Shapes features six different symbols, with their sizes varying from one card to another. Any two cards have exactly one symbol in common. To play, reveal one card, then another. Whoever spots the symbol in common on both cards claims the first card, then another card is revealed for players to search, and so on. Whoever has collected the most cards when the deck runs out wins!

Spot it! Alphabet plays similarly, but each card has eight items on it, thus making for a larger deck (55 cards vs. 31 cards) and more challenging play suitable for older players.

In addition to the base game, two additional games – each an observation game with a speed element – are included for each deck.
family: Characters: Jake and the Never Land Pirates

description:
Millions of years ago our ancestors a nomad race known as K'wertz S'teratz (“The Firsts”) reached our galaxy. They establish they colonies four planetary systems and gave birth to our people before gone. We lived centuries of peace and prosperity. But as it was predicted as the eras passed we became divided into four deeply different societies, four enemy factions. Our became starving for power, obsessed for domination. We started to insanely hate each other and now we only thing our once united societies thinks is about conquest. Each one built their own starship armada and declared war. Dreadful times came to our galaxy...May the K'wertz S'terats have mercy of our actions. Go to war my sons but remember: in the dark and cold space only death prevails.

Stellar Supremacy is a space combat game where 2 to 4 players struggle to domain the galaxy.

There are four ways to win the game: Map area control, Home Worlds Control, Victory Points or opponents elimination.

The game brings to the players strategical and tatical levels of action and the ability to combine game elements to implement them.

In Linnapeli, each player first draws a tile telling which castle(s) out of six possibilities is/are to be built. With 4–6 players each player builds one castle, with 3 players 2, and with 2 players 3 castles. Players then pick tiles and place them on 4*4 grid, trying to avoid errors in placing the tiles or taking tiles that do not belong to their castle(s). The first player to complete his/her castle(s) is the winner.

There are three variations to the gameplay:
1) The easy variation is to keep the common heap of tiles face-up, and the game box with pictures of the castles visible to the players.
2) The hard variation is to keep the common heap of tiles face-up but show the pictures of the castles only shortly in the beginning of the game.
3) The hardest variation is to keep the common heap of tiles face-down.

The castles to be built are Kastelholma, Turun linna, Viipurin linna, Kuusiston linna, Olavinlinna and Kajaanin linna.
A set of six appropriately-themed (crowd-funding based) cards, designed for backers of the title's campaign.

European Illuminati is a expansion set of eighteen new cards for the game Illuminati.  It adds European based background cards to the basic game.

First published in TSRs British magazine, Imagine (Issue 6 - Sep 1983), where the reader would need to constuct the cards by cutting out or photocopying the page of cards from the article.

The new cards includes; The Samaritans, The Vatican, Lions of Longleat, Irish Sectarian Army, The GLC, The Old School Tie, Dr Who and many more.

The memo game with sounds for children from 4 years. Players put a card in the board so that it can be 'read' by the computer chip. The machine than makes the appropriate sound e.g. use the cow card and the machine will "Moo".Then the child has to remember which button represents that sound. Collect a pre-set number of cards to win.

Wastelands: Tribes of the Apocalypse is set an unknown time after a thermonuclear and chemical war wiped out 90% of Earth’s population. Groups of nomadic wanderers, who somehow managed to survive, banded together to form tribes. Resources have become incredibly scarce, decontaminated resources even more so, and survival has become nigh-on-impossible.
There is, however, one last hope for survival.

NEW EDEN.

A bastion of hope, this fortified stronghold is the last refuge from the apocalyptic wastelands where, if radiation doesn't kill you, another tribe probably will. NEW EDEN is allowing sanctuary to one tribe, but to prove which tribe is the most worthy, an offering must be presented.

Offer what you can to NEW EDEN, and if your offering is the greatest, your tribe will be awarded refuge.

If not? Well, those cockroaches are looking delicious...

In Wastelands: Tribes of the Apocalypse each player is the  leader of a Tribe occupying the harsh Wastelands. The aim of the game is to gather resources which you can trade for various offerings on the Black Market. The player who has gathered the greatest offering to present to NEW EDEN gains entry into the city and wins the game.

The other players are doomed to an eternity roaming the desecrated remains of what was once Earth.

Expansions to Ortus Regni that add components for two additional players each. Each includes two additional full player card sets (green and gold Earls in the first, purple and orange Earls in the second) oak trays, painted cubes, and an extra Army card deck.

Ozymandias is a dice game of pyramidal deconstruction for two players using a Das Spiel set. Ozymandias has much in common with Rummy-style games, but attempts to minimize luck by using a dice pyramid as a source of matching.

The game consists of alternating turns consisting of two *takes*, except for the starting player who performs only a single take. A *take* is the act of removing some number of dice from the edges of the pyramid and scoring them according to their original orientation in the structure.

A player may take a group of dice from the pyramid according to certain scoring sets: runs, 3 of a kind, 4 of a kind, additive (numbers add to a sum), multiplicative (numbers multiply to a product), and others.  There are also takes that incur penalties, but that might be useful for setting up larger scoring sets.

The players will continue to take dice from the pyramid until either they both take negative scores on successive turns or all of the dice are removed from the pyramid. The player with the highest score at the end is the winner.

boardgame: The Good, The Bad & The Funny
description:
The Good, The Bad & The Funny is an Apples-style party card game for a bunch of people. Players make compound words or phrases from a set of word cards to satisfy a mission. The dealer decides which of the submissions is Good, which is Bad, and which is Funny, and rewards the players appropriately (3, -2 and 5 points respectively). The first to 21 (or -10) wins.

There are three main differences between The Good, The Bad & The Funny and other Apples-style games:

The three main differences are:

1. Scoring. Apples games with strangers can be aggravating - will they choose the funny card or the clever one? Or the realistic one? Or just choose something terrible.

The dealer always has to choose The Good, The Bad & The Funny.

The Good is the best representation of the mission card. It’s worth three points because, while good, it’s boring and obvious. And you probably had the perfect card, so whatever.

The Bad is the worst submission that round. It’s worth negative two because I’m sick of players constantly ditching cards instead of actually thinking about a good answer. You’ll also notice that many of the players that think they are funny will end up with a lot of bad answers.

The Funny gets the biggest laugh. Subjective, of course, but it’s worth five points for entertaining the room.

The first to (or above) 21 points wins, unless there is a tie, in which case play continues as normal until a single player has the highest score.

There’s another small twist, which is that players can also win by scoring -10 points. This makes it a bit more fun to be chosen for that bad answer in round 1.

2. Players always play two cards. This is a game about compound words and phrases. Our lives are surrounded by such combinations - you’ll start to notice much more after reading this sentence. Creating compound words and phrases is a wonderful challenge - it forces players to think about the many ways that a word can be interpreted and manipulated by another word. And the essence of this game is a carefully created wordlist that is composed of the most commonly occurring bits from compound words and phrases in the English language, almost all of which are nouns.

There are a couple of key benefits to this approach. Most answers are not reliant on a shared understanding of pop. Many other Apples games assume a certain level of 21st Century culture familiarity while the word cards from The Good, The Bad & The Funny are timeless (the mission cards, however, do have a bit of pop-infusion). Answers won’t get stale, as the likelihood of a one playing the same combination twice within a play session or many, many play sessions is infinitely low. Even playing the same word card won’t have the same negative effect as in other games because it is about how two cards react together, not simply a funny reference. Even if the same two cards are played together, the order can be changed to dramatically alter the meaning.

The game is very replayable without the need for many, many expansions.

3. At any given time, three mission cards are face up. Dealers get to choose which mission they want to judge, which allows for another layer of strategy. Players can hold onto cards that match future possible missions (don’t you hate throwing out the perfect card right before getting the matching mission?).

boardgame: Valentina: The Game – Tutto Crepax Vol. 1
description:
Valentina: The Game – Tutto Crepax Vol. 1 includes 96 color tiles and 16 subject cards featuring the art of Guido Crepax with two memory-style games included in the rules:

• In "Crazy Valentina" all players simultaneously try to find the six color tiles comprising their subject card in the pile of face-down tiles placed in a 12x8 grid. The first who finishes wins.

• In "Valentina Memory", players in turn search for color tiles, trying to find the six needed to cover their subject card.

The suggested age is not for the complexity of the games, but for the topics.

In Keyflower, each player develops her own unique village over four seasons by successfully bidding for village tiles that feature specialized buildings and boats, and by collecting skills, resources and workers ("keyples").

In Keyflower: The Merchants, the second expansion for Keyflower, players continue to develop their village by building extensions and cabins, entering into lucrative contracts, and encountering new combinations of items on the incoming boats.

Whereas Keyflower: The Farmers added breadth to the Keyflower game, Keyflower: The Merchants adds depth. The contracts, for example, provide more ways to score points, but may also be traded for the item they depict: workers, resources, or skill tiles. Extensions double the fixed point scores of existing tiles. Cabins provide more opportunities to upgrade and to build the extensions. Keyflower: The Merchants works well with 2-6 number of players and particularly enhances the two-player game.

Players may choose to use all of the tiles from Keyflower: The Merchants, then add additional tiles at random from Keyflower in order to make up the required number of tiles (the Merchant's variant), or they can simply combine the new tiles with the existing game.

boardgame: Fusilier: A Wargames System for the Flintlock Era
description:
A Wargames System for the Flintlock Era.

Fusilier is a wargames system designed for the period 1700 to 1815. Although it is recognised that flintlocks were used before and after these dates. It was mainly during this period that the flintlock musket and its associated socket-bayonet was the standard arm of the line infantry.

The system is designed to be used with any scale of figures. There is no formal figurescale, the unit sizes being dictated by the base sizes, which in turn are based on the frontages of the prototype units. An infantry battalion might be, for example, ten 25mm figures, twenty 15mm figures or fifty 5 mm figures.

The system is expressly designed for use with large formation games and is unsuitable for actions involving less than brigade strengths. Note that throughout the rules the term “formation” is used to convey the sense of brigade groupings and higher, whereas the term “unit” is used of bodies of troops making up brigades.

In Facility, a small group of invaders breaks into a well-guarded facility - skulking in the shadows, sneaking up behind guards and taking them down silently, dragging the bodies out of sight - with the goal of stealing valuable corporate secrets, or assassinating the enemy strategist, or sabotaging top-secret industrial production.

What's the catch? You don't control the team breaking in, you control the guards. Although 'control' isn't quite the word; you watch as your team follows their patrol routes, then - via a hand of cards - interject with that casual glance over the shoulder or decision to switch direction at a critical moment, to catch the intruders... off-guard.

Facility is a solo game that plays out as a cross between a deckbuilder and an AI-driven miniatures game. The player controls the actions of his guards by buying and playing cards from his deck, while the intruders follow a simple set of rules to sneak up behind the player's guards, knife them in the back and drag their bodies out of sight.

This spin off of the timeless game, Monopoly, is based off of the college town to Yellow Springs, Ohio.  The properties that can be purchased, tokens, and other game concepts are based off of actual locations and companies from the area that sponsored the production of the game.
This spin off of the classic board game, Monopoly, is based off of the York Candle company.
This spin off of the classic board game, Monopoly, is designed to primarily entertain younger children through the use of cartoon cars and vehicles which you must collect and put up for rent.
publishers: Late for the Sky Production Company

description:
This is the third of three mini-expansions for Petite Pastiche.  Only compatible with the "Petite Pastiche" edition, not with the first edition or international edition of Pastiche.

This expansion pack consists of 3 extra commission cards, with artworks by Edouard Manet, Sandro Botticelli, and Edgar Degas.

Adds the following three new commisions:
- "The Fifer" by Edouard Manet
- "Virgin and Child" by Sandro Botticelli
- "Dancer with Bouquet, Curtseying" by Edgar Degas
family: Theme: Fine Art and Art Museums

description:
You are going to cry!

Cartrollen  is a fast paced racing game where the players compete aggressively to be the first to finish a racing adventure that takes place all across the world, thus composed of different road types, where events and attacks occur and objects might be used.

The adventure track consists of 10 randomly selected road segments with different characteristics, like jungle, swamp or highway, and the players need to race to see who is the first one to get to the end.

A player, in his turn, throws 2d6 to get Action Points that can be spent in either buying or playing cards, travelling to the next road segment, repairing their car or refuelling. When travelling, fuel is spent. Cards can either be objects that you can update your car with, attacks to other players, special cards like "counterattacks" or general events that can be thrown to other players (or yourself) so they happen to them, either good or bad.
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

description:
In this game you are a team of divers who have to have to go from the upper left corner, around the board to the upper right corner. Along the way you encounter different things:
There are eight red spots; if your diver land there you will be skipped - if another player is on the red field you land on, he is sent back to Start.
On one of the 10 white spots different things occour, it could be that you have to move x spots back/foward.

Each player have three divers. The first to get his three divers to the upper right corner is the winner.

Objective: Using the detailed street map of Cologne each player is given five companies (each divided in 4 pieces/tiles) to be established in the city as close to the centre as possible.

With the help of dice and counters, each players chooses where to place, turn or remove company tiles.
Once a company is established on the map, the corresponding four 'places worth seeing' are revealed, so familiarizing players with the sights of the city.
remarks:
game approach is identical with the 'cityprogame' family; game production was financed by advertisements of locally based companies

Der Brennstoff Boss is a economic game about the trading of fuel oil, coal and browncoal. Every player has the role of an merchant of fluid fuel or fossil burning. After several rounds the player wins who had earns the most money during the trading.
The player has to look for a good price at the distributer and must get the best price from his customers.

It's a promotional game from Rheinbraun Company, a german trading company for coal, browncoal and fuel oil.

Inspired by a certain sabre wielding hero from 8 bit computer games of the 1980's, 8-bit Jungle Hero is an adventure game played on a map that changes throughout play.

The object of the game is to navigate the labyrinthine jungle paths of the lost valley, surviving the encounters within. Adventurers must locate the lost pieces of an amulet and then make it to the magic portal that will allow them to escape. At the same time travelers must avoid the predations of “The Beast” a terrible creature who patrols the valley.

Each turn players explore their surroundings, react to encounters, change the map of the valley and move "The Beast"

The map is a grid of tiles with four fixed points - mountains, within it.  As part of their turn players get the opportunity to rotate or move one or more of the tiles of the map, changing the paths through the jungle.  Players also have a short trail of tiles immediately behind them that they have already visited. This moves with them and can change in length depending on the items they are carrying.  This path cannot be changed by other players.  However if "The Beast" picks up this trail it will follow it and may attack the unfortunate adventurer.

Each time they enter a new tile players draw from an encounter deck.  This may produce a beneficial result but more often will reveal one of the jungle denizens poised to attach them.  Surviving an encounter may result in them getting treasure, including pieces of the amulet needed to escape the valley.

Escape from the valley is via a portal that moves around the outside edge of the map each turn.  To escape players must move off the edge of the map onto this portal

A game on the conflict in this province of southern Afghanistan, 2008-10. Players take the role of regional commanders (Afghan National Security Forces and Taliban, yes not the ISAF) striving for the resources to allow them to earn Victory Points, which are granted in accordance with objectives set them by the same higher authorities that provide them with those resources. Players will find themselves in the position of having, if they wish to continue to get high levels of support, to follow courses of action that are maybe not the most effective in opposing the enemy but are more valued by their superiors, and which themselves change from time to time during the game. When you run out of support, the game ends – the war continues but with a different regional commander!

An ambitious development of the Algeria/Shining Path etc. family game system. Menus of kinetic and non-kinetic missions, with optional added complexity in the form of additional factions (an intelligence/counterintelligence system, organized crime, tribal militias, additional powers for ISAF forces). One 11×17″ area-movement map of Kandahar province, 140 double-sided counters.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Probably the most exciting football game ever created and simple.

It will celebrate a vibrant football game in which each participant will be putting the pieces of "field" turns forming a puzzle. They may strategically deploy its defenders, midfielders, and forwards coach to make plays in order to make "GOAL".

Score is not an easy task, since it is necessary to attach a piece ball with another piece with a goal through sections of path and who has, of course, a player inside.

description:
It's election season in Gomorrah, and it's not politics as usual.  Citizens have gathered in the Town Council to vote for mayor.  Huckster Wilbur Crowley faces off against mad scientist Roderick Byre.  Of course, each Outfit in town has their own interests at stake as well.

Bijen Race ('Bee Race') is a small boardgame for children of 5 and up.  In this game players need to put their tokens in a specific order that is determined by shaken a beehive.

The beehive is shaken and 6 colored balls fall out. Now players need to put the colored tokens in their possesion in the exact order and push the beehive. First player to do so gets a point. As soon as one player collects 3 points they win the game.

boardgame: Stay and See Georgia: It's a PEACH of a game
description:
Players move around a map of the state of Georgia leaning fun historical facts about the state while racing to visit eight locations first.  The game contains a map of Georgia, 100 location cards, four small colored cars, 32 green chips and four peach tree cards (with eight peaches on each tree).

Players begin the game by choosing a car and taking 8 green chips and a peach tree card showing 8 peaches.  The players then draw a card from the location deck and place their car at that place.  That card is then placed at the bottom of the deck.  The players then draw a new card, reading it aloud to all.  This will be their first location that they will need to travel to.

Players roll a die and move their car, trying to reach the location on their card.  When they do, they place a green chip on a peach on their tree, draw a new card and repeat journey on to the next location.  The winner is the first person to reach eight locations, covering all their peaches with green chips.

(Information is from rules on the back of the game box.)

As you can see in Images, Patrick Tremoureux has his beautiful professional renditions of the map and counters.

Dice must die is a quick, abstract, solitaire dice rolling game

In the ancient world, civilizations struggle against their enemies using the strength of their numbers, the hardness of bronze and iron, and the powers granted to them by fickle gods. Plagues, famines, and fire are suffered or inflicted while soldiers march to war in the name of the deities they serve. Only the civilization with the strongest pantheon will prevail.

Pantheon is a card game for 2-5 players created by veteran game designer Timothy Brown (co-creator of the Dark Sun campaign setting), developed by Jamie Chambers with graphic design by Digger Hayes, featuring art by Lindsay Archer, Michael Bielaczyc, Melanie Hayes, Nick Kremenek, and Joie Brown.

In Pantheon you start with a fledgling ancient culture. Grow your population, build temples, advance your civilization, and wage war on your enemies! There are many paths to victory, but when the game ends only the strongest pantheon will triumph. It features gods from ancient Greek, Norse, Egyptian, Chinese, and Indian mythology.

boardgame: WOMBAT! The game of Word Combat!
description:
WOMBAT! is the one-upping, buzzer-beating, shoutout showdown countdown smackdown game of Word Combat!

A card is revealed showing a letter or combination of letters. Now, the players start shouting out words that contain that letter.

After a while, new cards are added. Players now have to shout words that contain all the letters.

The player that shouts the last word before the timer runs out, wins the round and scores the points ("donuts") printed on the letter cards.

12 minutes to play
2 to 20 players, age 7+
Everyone plays at once
New folks can jump in at any time
Easy enough for kids
Rich enough for serious word gamers

Air... and the equal Librium gravity are shut down. D.A.V.E. is now abusing the gravity system to throw you around the ship in order to stop you from doing anything productive.

boardgame: Galactic Overlords: A Space Empire Strategy Game
description:
In Galactic Overlords, the players are challenged to guide their race from a small beginning on a rich home world and grow it into a powerful space empire.  This is a full-fledged space empire game featuring exploration, expansion, exploitation of galactic resources AND space combat.  However, it eschews the extermination or elimination of weaker players by allowing the stronger ones to become their overlords, thus extending to them a measure of protection.

The order of game play is on a round system where players do their empire production simultaneously in a production phase.  Then the players do two actions each, in turn order, in the action phase.  In their actions, players may engage their opponents by diplomacy, trade, and espionage as well as military ventures.  Or they may go off on their own to explore for rewarding encounters.

The building of economic engines will not be enough to win.  There is a shifting economy and there are changes to game conditions with the passing of ages.  Players must be willing to abandon hard-won colonies in order to adapt to the changes.  The game can be customized in many ways, one being the duration of the game can be set to any number of rounds the players want.  Players can set limits on the aggression level of the game if they desire a more peaceful experience.  There is a four player introductory game designed to introduce players to the basics of the game.  In this "intro" game, the special alien abilities and technologies are not used, thus placing players on an equal footing.

A variety of mechanisms are employed which are designed to quash the common complaints that tend to occur in multiplayer conflict games.

There are several possible ways the game can end in addition to timing out on the last round.  Players are rewarded on a point system for their accomplishments such their as economy, technology, exploration, military, cultural and diplomatic achievements.  The highest score gains the win.

"Step right up ladies and gents and witness the eighth wonder of the world! Flip Florey's Flying Flea-rina! These special Argentinian fleas have been trained in the arts of martial combat and acrobatics for your entertainment and edification!  Watch them perform amazing acts of aerialist acumen. See them fight and spar as the ancient gladiators of lore. Experience a singular event of epic and microscopic proportions. Flip Florey's Flying Flea-rina will amaze and astound you!"

In Flea-rina, the players compete with their fleas to perform amazing stunts while making glorious attacks against each other. Each turn, the players select a stunt with a movement path. They then roll dice trying to match the icons on the stunt. Completing the stunt reaps victory points by entertaining the crowd. If they fail to complete the stunt they may still move along the path up to the icons rolled and may collect resources off the board and/or attack the other fleas. Fighting fleas also entertain the the crowd, gaining victory points with gladiatorial skill.

When the players have depleted all the resources on the board or when one flea has been defeated, the game is over and the player who has entertained the crowd the most wins.

A simple game published about the German City of Herford.

During each player's turn they roll a six-sided dice to move along a game-board depicting a map of Essen. At certain spaces players can acquire cards of the shops or they can go sightseeing by following the instructions on the back of the card. Sometimes there is only information written, sometimes you have to pay an amount of money or at other times you may earn some cash.

The game ends when the last player reaches the finish space with his playing piece. The winner is the person who has acquired the most sightseeing and/or shop cards.

A solitaire game where you play as Captain Oscar Locke, a stranded pirate, whose luck just turned around. Another crew of pirates has landed on the island where you were stranded by your mutinous ex-crew. This new crew of pirates are here to bury their treasure; leaving their boat unmanned just off the coast. You could try to man the ship yourself but with no gold to spend, what's the point? Capture the treasure before it's buried and make it to the ship to sail the high seas with your newly claimed booty.

In this game of exploration, players will use various resources coupled with dice rolling to battle the pirates and animals they will encounter as they move throughout the island. If players can manage their inventory and time, they might be able to get off the island alive.

boardgame: Sentinels of the Multiverse: The Prime Wardens Promo Pack
description:
A team that was formed to aid a neophyte Virtuoso of the Void against Akash'Bhuta, the Prime Wardens were led by The Argent Adept in that conflict.

A stalwart companion and a fierce defender, Haka was quick to take up the mantle of Prime Warden. He saw the team as a chance to help protect even more people. Haka has seen more than his fair share of years and experienced more loss than most could comprehend, but among his powerful allies, he finds a sort of peace in fighting to save the world.

After years of going from extreme to extreme, Fanatic finally found some moderation, and her allies in the Prime Wardens had no small part in helping the avenging angel center herself. Now, she stands more resolute than ever - not for vengeance or for zealotry, but for the defense of the innocent and the advancement of the greater good.

The alien who is a stranger in a land which offers unfamiliarity and persecution, Tempest has gone from a creature on the run to a well-respected hero and savior for both the Maerynian people and the human race. As a member of the Prime Wardens, Tempest seeks to save all those who would stand for what is right, and fights those who would oppress and destroy.

A defender of his homeland and of the entire world, Captain Cosmic was a natural addition to the Prime Wardens. He both protects and arms his allies with his constructs, and his innate connection to the cosmos rounds out well this team of powerful heroes.

Whenever threats great in power and scale threatened the world, the Prime Wardens would form once again - a team of mighty heroes each wielding considerable power!

Sentinels of the Multiverse: The Prime Wardens brings 5 alternate hero cards. These promo versions of The Argent Adept, Haka, Fanatic, Tempest, and Captain Cosmic are used with their respective hero decks from your Sentinels of the Multiverse collection.

boardgame: Sentinels of the Multiverse: Omnitron IV Environment
description:
After the cosmically imbued Omnitron was destroyed by the heroes of earth, many thought the last had been seen of the destructive artificial intelligence. Baron Blade merely saw potential. He worked through many channels to recover parts of the Omnitron frame, and rebuilt and reactivated them as his own personal Omni-Blade!
 However, after the Vengeful Five's defeat, there was enough sentience left in the Omni-Blade that it knew to hide. It slowly accumlated parts and resources, but it had lost much of its intelligence. It struggled to reach its former power, but it was missing too much of itself. Instead, it formed a massive construction assemblary. There, it pumped out drones and devices, hoping to match in numbers what it once had in power.

Many seeking power have attempted to harness the automatically generated troops and resources created by the fourth incarnation of Omnitron. Heroes have faced more than one villain in the self-aware factory, each attempting to join forces with the robot, or even to subjugate its programming. If any were to succeed, the results would be disastrous...

description:
The harvest in the year of 1284 was really abundant: The granaries and storerooms of the city of Hamelin are bursting with food, thus  the four great rat tribes of the city race to be the first to gather all kinds of provisions for the winter to come...but they did not expect the arrival of the Pied Piper whose job as a rat-cathcer is to exterminate the vermin by the song of his magical flute...

Players take turns by rolling two dice in order to reach a food token: one dice is for movement, the other triggers a good or bad event (i.e. procreation of new rats, teleport home or Pied Piper's song (kills all rats in streets) or summon the Pied Piper's cat (blocks a door). Afterwards the food token must be returned to your home base.  The aim is to block other players' paths and be quicker than the others to deliver food to the own base or just steal from other players. The first to collect four different tokens wins the game.

boardgame: Sentinels of the Multiverse: Wager Master Villain Character
description:
Before time was time, before space was space, there was the wager. The wager that nothing would happen.

That wager was lost. And from that loss came the Wager Master.

As reality formed and matter came into being, all of the losing wagers - all of the impossible possibilities - also formed into being. Into a specific being: the Wager Master.

The Wager Master appears however it wants. It commonly goes for centuries without showing up anywhere in the Multiverse. But when it does show up, it’s a safe bet that things will go wrong. The Wager Master thrives on playing with the rules of reality. It appears to a race of sentient beings, plays games with whatever champions they might have, and ultimately destroys everything it encounters as they inevitably lose the Wager Master’s games. Those who lose to the Wager Master become mere playing pieces for more twisted games.

Now, unfortunately, the capricious Wager Master has appeared on earth. Too many things have gone wrong. Too many coincidences and too many confluences point to the truth: we are all pawns in the cruel game of an entity as old as the Multiverse. What chance do the Sentinels of that Multiverse have?

---

description:
In The Fall. Game Of Winds you are Gods of Wind blowing on a Balloon in order to make it land safely or push it against an obstacle during its fall.
Most of you are Friendly Winds, whose goal is to help the Balloon land safely; the others are Adverse Winds, whose goal is to hinder the Balloon during its fall, causing it to collide with an obstacle or to fall into the water.
During each turn you make your secret play by choosing a card, then all players unveil it at the same time “blowing on the balloon” while it falls (i.e. moving it accordingly to the cards played while advancing a turn). 
If you are a Friendly Winds you will try to guess who your mates are and match your plays in order to hold the control of the fall. If you are an Adverse Winds you will try to hide and hit at the right moment, causing a spectacular crash. 
Most important, no matter how the games ends, there will always be the time for another challenge: the playing time is less than 20 minutes, the game is pocket-sized so you may play it almost everywhere, the random matching of players guarantees a good longevity, both as party game or evening filler.
Despite its simple rules, the game is hard to master. Strategies have to take into account the possible actions of other players. Predicting what they will do is a key factor to win.
